responseSet.title: On: Where are you, my Beloved? On Absence, Loss and the Enigma of Telepathic Dreams
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

On: Where are you, my Beloved? On Absence, Loss and the Enigma of Telepathic Dreams

Moran Shoham

Moran Shoham

Sport St. 18, Haifa, Israel — moran9898@hotmail.com
13 April 2007

Moran Shoham  

Dear Editor

I would like to say a few words about Dr. Eshel's paper (2006), about its value and its place. It might be easier to begin with its place. For Stevens (1999), the poem originates from the fact that we live in a world that is not our own and is not of ourselves (see ‘Notes toward a supreme fiction': ‘From this the poem springs: that we live in a place/ That is not our own and, much more, not ourselves/ And hard it is in spite of blazoned days'). Eshel's paper originates where Stevens poetry originates: from an odd combination of terror, awe and wonderment. Its theme is unusual only in so far as we are in such need of the usual to comfort and console us. The author records what has taken place. She tells us that she does not know what to make of things but that she does know this much: that they happened. And her report is saturated with astonishment, wonder and uneasiness verging on a deeper discomfort. It is this capacity for openness to experience and for its registration that gives the paper its value, I think. Beyond topic or theme, the author shares an attitude that might be characterized as daring, one that goes with the registration of facts before they are shaped into a matrix of understanding of any sort or kind. And in a subtle but consistent manner Eshel manages to sustain this very attitude towards her theme: she is not suggesting mysticism, nor was she personally transformed. But she does relate a personal capacity, almost a need I would say, to dare and face things with openness of mind, which, in turn, opens the way for the undercurrents of amazement and awe which, to my mind, are this paper's deeper theme.

Bion transformed our notion of projective identification into a larger vision of the way we intuit ourselves into one another and into relationships, a realization that Eshel seems to appreciate fully. That we reach towards each other in ways that baffl e and surpass our understanding is thus the given out of which the author grafts the explanation of ‘absent-within-absence', itself a poetic response to a sense of a beyond.

responseSet.title: Hunger and Love: Schiller and the Origin of Drive Dualism in Freud's Work1
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Hunger and Love

Schiller and the Origin of Drive Dualism in Freud's Work1

Patricia Cotti

Patricia Cotti

26 rue de Paradis, F-75480, Paris CEDEX 10, France—pat-ricia@wanadoo.fr
(Final version accepted 24 March 2006)

Patricia Cotti  

JCF von Schiller, S Freud, drive dualism, food drive, sexual drive, interpretation of dreams, theory of seduction

The interpretation of dreams was the first text in which Freud referred to the system of two drives (drive of self-preservation and the sexual drive). In order to understand how this question was at work in Freud's mind, one has to go back to 1898, when Freud began to write the third chapter of The interpretation of dreams. One can then see, in contrast with Sulloway's assertions, how Freud was inspired by Schiller, whose shadow haunted his dreams between April and December 1898. The analysis of these dreams emphasizes how the references to Schiller's works and to the drive of self-preservation cover sexual impulses, in particular, those connected with the relationship to the father. The food drive or drive of self-preservation also enabled Freud to construct a heroic romance. He was thereby able to bury an internal criticism which was at odds with his persistence in describing the father as a seducer, and to conceal scenes in which he was defeated and sexually subdued by another boy.

responseSet.title: Morning Sessions
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Morning Sessions

Juan Brandt

Juan Brandt

NGO Section, Department of Public Information, UN Headquarters, New York, NY 10017, USA—brandt@un.org

Juan Brandt  

An audience of over 250 attended, and three presenters spoke.

Cláudio Laks Eizirik (Porto Alegre), President of the International Psychoanalytical Association (IPA), and one of the leaders in the study of cultural factors influencing personality development, delivered a psychoanalytic perspective. He noted Freud's contributions to the understanding of the human mind and human behavior. Highlighting the internal conflict between love and aggression that may lead to expressions of hatred and violence, he especially noted early upbringing in the fostering of aggression or the capacity for love, the quality of the mother-child relationship and the presence of a father figure. The community's ability to hold and assist the family unit is crucial; a lack of social structures may lead to significant damage to the unit. In many severely traumatized individuals, unresolved mourning may lead to a deficit in the ability to symbolize. These unmetabolized, unsymbolized mental structures are then transmitted to future generations. These can be thought of as part of unconscious transgenerational transmission of hate, war and violence. Dr. Eizirik stressed the distinction between intergenerational and transgenerational transmission of psychic trauma. Intergenerational transmission is conscious and utilizes such processes as fantasy formation and identification. It is organized into a family narrative that is successively passed from one generation to the next. In contract, transgenerational transmission refers to unconscious mental content passed on that is dissociated, primitive and un-integrated. This kind of transmission is not symbolized in words or through stories.

[[RunningHead]]

Tonårstid. Utveckling, problem och psykoterapeutisk behandling[The teenage years. Development, problems and psychotherapeutic treatment] by Björn Wrangsjö and Majlis Winberg Salomonsson Falköping: Natur och Kultur. 2006. 397 p.

Bernadette Buhl-Nielsen

Bernadette Buhl-Nielsen

Guldborgvej 17, DK-4000 Roskilde, Denmark — bb-n@dadlnet.dk

Bernadette Buhl-Nielsen  

As adults we want the best for our children. (p. 7)

A deceptively simple statement forms the opening of this rich and thought-provoking work. Wrangsjö and Salomonsson have tackled the task of writing a book on teenagers that both has broad appeal and treats the subject matter with depth and insight. Running throughout is a deep respect and understanding of teenagers and the problems they must tackle. In the spirit of Erikson (1950), they attempt to understand the teenagers' search for meaning and identity. The theoretical backbone of the book is psychoanalytic: the authors draw on a wide variety of writers on adolescence, including Erikson, Laufer and Blos. Their application of the work of Daniel Stern (1985) to the understanding of adolescence is refreshing and innovative. The book also combines insights from an understanding of family therapy. A psychiatric approach and neurobiological understanding of mental illness are part of the integrated approach. Not content with this balancing act, the authors also manage to bring in insights from sociology.

The division of the book into five parts, each of which can be read in isolation but which profit from being read and understood in context, helps to structure the otherwise complex balancing act between different disciplines. The first part of the book looks at the social context of the adolescent, and the second at development from child to young adult. The third and fourth parts go into detail about the evaluation, diagnosis and psychodynamic treatment of adolescents, and form the backbone of the work. The final part of the book looks at combinations of treatments and points to future developments in the field.

The first part describes the complex social situation in which teenagers find themselves.

responseSet.title: La fascinación del líder: En torno a un libro olvidado de S. Freud sobre el Presidente Wilson [The fascination with the leader: On a forgotten book by S. Freud about President Wilson] by Fanny Elman Schutt Madrid: Biblioteca Nueva. 2005. 140 p.
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

La fascinación del líder: En torno a un libro olvidado de S. Freud sobre el Presidente Wilson[The fascination with the leader: On a forgotten book by S. Freud about President Wilson] by Fanny Elman Schutt Madrid: Biblioteca Nueva. 2005. 140 p.

Gracia Daimiel

Gracia Daimiel

c/ Alfonso X, 3, 3°a, E-28010 Madrid, Spain

Gracia Daimiel  

Written in 1930-2, the psychological study of President Wilson (Freud and Bullitt, 1967) has never become part of the Freud complete works. It is this exclusion that

1Translated by Judith Filc.800

aroused the curiosity of Fanny Elman Schutt (a psychoanalyst with extensive experience of teaching Freudian thought), leading her to embark on a long and rigorous study that culminates in this book.

Why has this text been so neglected? Why was it excluded from Freud's complete works? Why did Freud collaborate with Bullitt, a man so foreign to the psychoanalytic milieu? What was Freud's actual contribution, and what was Bullitt's? Such are the questions the author tackles, offering us her own answers in a clear and simple language, always from the perspective of Freud's oeuvre and without losing sight of the sociopolitical context in which the text was written. To do so, she had to painstakingly trace not only Freud's contribution, but also the details of the agreement both authors reached in Berlin in 1930, the fantastic itinerary of the manuscript—always in Bullitt's hands—the vicissitudes of its publication, and its very cool reception.

The book—preceded by a thorough and interesting presentation by Maricarmen Jiménez (Professor of Psychology, Barcelona Central University)—starts with ‘Wilson, a psychological study’. In this first chapter, Elman Schutt provides clarifying and indispensable historical information on the protagonists and their sociopolitical context—the Great War and the Versailles peace conference—which takes up a significant part of the original text. The author also investigates Freud's co-author's personal background, as well as his participation and responsibility in the loss of the original manuscript. Oddly, this loss took place on the same day the Germans occupied Paris, when Bullitt was the US ambassador to France.

The second chapter, ‘Ideas and motivations’, doubtless constitutes the core of the book. Based on her knowledge and understanding of Freud's ideas (which enable her to reveal the complex theoretical framework that supports them), the author shows, after a thorough study, the phrases that belong to Freud's ‘own hand’. She takes them out of context and differentiates them from those that do not carry his mark in terms of either style or conceptual lineage. With the sagacity of a detective and the curious gaze of a researcher, Elman Schutt ascertains that some of these phrases, diluted in the totality of the text, converge on ‘the question of the leader’, particularly of the leader ‘who can fascinate, intoxicating his listeners with words and leading them to lose their ability to recognize facts that are adverse to him in the real world’ (Freud and Bullitt, 1967, pp. 63, 219). She is struck by the fact that this is not President Wilson's profile, since he became a world leader circumstantially due to the expectations of peace he generated at the end of World War I. That is why she advances the hypothesis that what influenced Freud's decision to accept Bullitt's proposal to write a book with him—a politician—was the social climate at the time. A leader such as Hitler—a leader so fatal for the history of humankind—had appeared on the horizon of an educated nation such as Germany and was rising unceasingly. Elman Schutt considers then the possibility that Wilson's figure might have served Freud to develop and publish ideas that had been stirring in his mind and were reactivated by the sociopolitical context. These ideas had started to emerge in Group psychology and the analysis of the ego (Freud, 1921) written during equally distressing times.

responseSet.title: Contemporary psychoanalysis in America: Leading analysts present their work Edited by Arnold M. Cooper Washington, DC: American Psychiatric Publishing. 2006. 759 p.
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Contemporary psychoanalysis in America: Leading analysts present their work Edited by Arnold M. Cooper Washington, DC: American Psychiatric Publishing. 2006. 759 p.

Maria Ponsi

Maria Ponsi

via della Chiesa 77, 50125 Firenze, Italy — mariaponsi@dada.it

Maria Ponsi  

These days psychoanalysis, in spite of enjoying apparent good health due to the large volume of studies and researches being produced, is also afflicted by the serious epis-temological problem of lacking a corpus of basic assumptions. It is well known that any term or concept in the psychoanalytic vocabulary doesn't have the same meaning for psychoanalysts of different persuasions. Behind terms and concepts, there are different ways of conceiving mental functioning and the psychoanalytic process.

Nonetheless, psychoanalytic knowledge does evolve. However, it does not develop around a core of shared knowledge; instead, separate islands proliferate and grow larger—unfortunately, only occasionally do the islands join up with one another. So, whoever wanted to survey the wide assortment of new ideas circulating in the psychoanalytic community by means of a collection of writings would be in a tight spot. First of all, such an editor would select papers that were as broadly representative as possible, trying to reduce bias to a minimum. Moreover, to avoid turning a collection of writings into a potpourri in which the reader gets lost, the editor usually groups texts into specific subject area sections. This is not an easy task either, since any criterion one chooses to employ—e.g. model or school, topic (clinical, theoretical, empirical, historical, conceptual research, methodology, applied psychoanalysis, interdisciplinary studies, and so on)—would turn out to be unsatisfying, as many articles deal with a variety of subjects and hence could be put simultaneously in different sections.

Arnold Cooper, an eminent psychoanalyst whose writings bear the imprint of American pragmatism, has assembled a collection that avoids these difficulties. He did not attempt to choose himself the most representative contemporary papers, but devolved the task to 30 renowned American psychoanalysts by asking them to recommend contributions they thought best represented their own current thinking and major contribution. Moreover, he abandoned the idea of splitting the book into sections, since he realized that many of the papers crossed school and topic boundaries. The result is an anthology with a very simple format. The papers are presented alphabetically by author and every text is preceded by a thorough, but not overlong, biographical note written by the editor, and by two short notes written by each author, one pertaining to the main interests he/she has been involved in during the course of his/her professional life and the other about the reason why the choice has fallen on that particular paper as representative of his/her ideas.

[[RunningHead]]

André Green: Associations (presque) libres d'un psychanalyste Entretiens avec Maurice Corcos [(Almost) Free associations of a psychoanalyst: Interview with Maurice Corcos] by Maurice Corcos Paris: Albin Michel. 2006. 345 p.

Eléana Mylona

Eléana Mylona

4 rue des Chartreux, 75006 Paris, France

Eléana Mylona  

In this book composed of interviews conducted at an allegro vivace pace, André Green gives us a panoramic view of his psychoanalytic writings, marked by major societal, artistic, scientific and human issues, on the one hand, and by his analytic practice, on the other.

In my opinion, this book is a perfect illustration of the intertwining of the intraanalytic and the extra-analytic; here, psychoanalysis regains its Freudian dialectical co-ordinates, originally guaranteeing its lasting interest. In effect, André Green sets up a lively dialogue between psychoanalysis, literature, theatre, philosophy, psychiatry and the neurosciences; between the psyche and the other essential components of the human.

‘But who is this man whose entire oeuvre is a praise of transmission?’ (p. 14). This is the question Maurice Corcos, psychiatrist and hospital practitioner, sets out to answer in the course of his interviews with André Green. In fact, Green's answers seem to illustrate the intimate relation between these two questions, one about the man and the other about his oeuvre, as this relation develops through and in his analytic practice. The latter determines his reading of his own life and culture, his interpretation of knowledge about the human and of the history of psychoanalysis in France, as well as the elaboration of his theory.

Green's dialogue with a psychiatrist, particularly one with a psychoanalytic culture, makes for a suspenseful confrontation. From the start, the reader is invited to consider the double question: how are psychoanalytic practice and psychiatric practice alike and different? They are brought face to face in order to expose their similarities and differences, in order for each one to define itself, bringing to light the main lines of Green's thought.

1322

[[RunningHead]]

Le parole ritrovate. Le parole dalle immagini. Rifl essioni teoriche e percorsi analitici dalle psicosi[Words rediscovered, words from images: Psychosis: theoretical refl ections and analytic approaches] by Marina Breccia Roma: Borla. 2006. 232 p.

Livia Tabanelli

Livia Tabanelli

Via di Villa Chigi 37, I - 00199 Rome, Italy — livia.tabanelli@tin.it

Livia Tabanelli  

The author of this work, Marina Breccia, is an Italian psychiatrist and psychoanalyst who lives and works near Pisa, in Tuscany, where she has also worked for the regional psychiatric services. The book opens with an introduction by Domenico Chianese, which highlights, appropriately, the way in which the text, while focusing on the issue of psychosis, cannot confi ne itself solely to a discussion of this subject. At the heart of the book is an exploration of the image, and the relationship between image and word, between image and thought—an exploration which seeks out the connections which allow the transition from one to the other.

Apart from refl ecting on the treatment of psychosis, this author invites us to think about the use of images in contemporary culture. Whereas psychoanalysis holds that it is important for psychic health to be able to use the image as a point of departure from which to rediscover individual and creative thought, very often in the world today, images are used for denial—they are an ‘excellent example of splitting in its most developed form’ (p. 20). One image is replaced so quickly by the next that there is no time to acknowledge difference and thus no space for thought. Breccia writes,

…we may be remembering those who died in the Nazi concentration camps, but all it takes is one click, the length of time to change from one channel to another, and an image change takes place, whereby, seamlessly, we have been moved into another world and are now presented with a banquet of images of the news or another TV show …. Everything is reduced to 0 time … the banquet thus erases death and destruction. (p. 20)

The book is divided into three parts. In the fi rst part, ‘Desire: A starting point’, the author takes us on a journey that, starting with Freud, also develops various aspects of the theories of desire and the symbol put forward by Lacan, Aulagnier and Assoun. In the second part, Laplanche and Pontalis's concept of fantasme is revisited, after which the author sets out her own position in the chapters entitled ‘Reconstructing the word out of the image’ and ‘Metaphor: The location of the movement from image to word’. The third part opens with the chapter ‘Time, memory, repetition and the possible role of analysis’, in which Green's thoughts on the temporal nature of the psyche and of the analytic cure are considered. The following chapters return to the place where the author gained her experience: the consulting room. In particular, there is an impassioned and ‘painful’ description of the cases of two young women, who started in analysis after having been admitted to hospital following severe psychotic episodes. The description of the clinical material is useful and moving.

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Figure del dialogo tra antropologia e psicoanalisi [Figures of the dialogue between anthropology and psychoanalysis] by Alfredo Lombardozzi Roma: Borla. 2006. 200 p.

Giovanna Goretti

Giovanna Goretti

Via Panama 114, I-00198 Roma, Italy — goretti.regazzoni@libero.it

Giovanna Goretti  

In the interview-dialogue by Claudio Neri with Alfredo Lombardozzi—the last chapter of this book and happy occasion for a new narration in dialogic terms of many subjects considered in the book and for a substantial theoretical framing of many authors mentioned there—Neri asks why it is interesting to read the book and who could read it with profit.

In his answer Lombardozzi, an anthropologist conquered by psychoanalysis, a psychoanalyst who keeps feeding on anthropology, points out, in his double identity (a theme, the identity, which will be central in the book), the possibility to look at the two disciplines from within. This look from within could help psychoanalysts who deal with social and cultural facts to avoid ‘fruitless generalizations and misunderstandings’ (p. 191) and could foster in anthropologists a greater sensibility for inner life and maybe a more open and friendly attitude towards psychoanalysis itself. To the cultured person who is not particularly versed in either one or the other discipline, Lombardozzi feels like offering a reconsideration about the relation man-culture and man-nature and wishes to convey that passionate element which he believes fundamental in his reflection.

The introduction, rather than offering a perspective view on the content of the book, introduces the reader to one of the privileged areas of intersection between psychoanalysis and anthropology, the theme of the sacred. This is a theme of great complexity with which Lombardozzi deals starting from Freud's works, with the support of numerous (too many?) theoretical references, going through the manifold and sometimes antithetical articulations within the two fields, requiring the reader to follow him through the theories of different thinkers, belonging to different periods and to different geographical areas. ‘A coming back home’ (for the psychoanalyst reader) is, in the last pages of the chapter, a reference to the current valorization of religion proposed by relational theories which recognize the utility of faith for man on the basis of a different role acknowledged to illusion and on the basis of the function of cultural self-objects—among which religion can be included—for the narcissistic balance.

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Psicoanalisi e neuroscienze [Psychoanalysis and neuroscience] Edited by Mauro Mancia Milan: Springer. 2007. 460 p.

Luca Imeri

Luca Imeri

Via Mangiagalli 32, I-20133 Milan, Italy — luca.imeri@unimi.it

Luca Imeri  

Thanks to developments in the field of neuroscience, has the time come for psychoanalysis to find neurobiological bases? And, on the other hand, can

1573

psychoanalytical models of the mind help neuroscientists (in the broad meaning of the term) in their work, which is now challenging new fields and getting closer and closer to mental functions? This new volume, edited by Mauro Mancia, a psychoanalyst and neurophysiologist, offers chapters that describe the new neuro-scientific developments that are of direct relevance to psychoanalysis together with chapters by psychoanalysts who start reflecting on what these findings can mean to their practice and to the theories which are at the basis of that practice. The volume is organized in four sections, devoted to (i) memory (and its relationship to the unconscious); (ii) brain systems for the internal representation of actions and states of others; (iii) dream; and (iv) the development of mental activity in the foetus and infant.

Memory, which is of crucial interest to both neuroscience and psychoanalysis, also represents the field where the dialogue between the two disciplines has the widest common ground and can prove to be most useful for both. Neuroscience has always investigated explicit (or declarative) memory, which encompasses episodic and semantic memories, is conscious, can be verbalized and recalled. Only recently, neuroscience started devoting attention to priming and procedural memory (learning, for instance, to run a bicycle or play an instrument), which are now considered part of a ‘new’ form of memory, defined as implicit memory, which is non-conscious and cannot be verbalized. Associative (such as classical conditioning) and non-associative (such as habituation) learning are also part of implicit memory and have been the subject of neuroscientific investigation for a long time. What neuroscientists have almost never investigated so far is another dimension of implicit memory, affective and emotional memory.

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Letters to The Editors

Reply to Dr Rose

Elliot Jurist

Elliot Jurist

138th St/Convent Av, NAC Building, Room 8/109, New York, NY 10031, USA—EJurist@gc.cuny.edu
26 November 2006

Elliot Jurist  

Dear Sirs,

Dr. Rose would like his work in the area of psychoanalysis and aesthetics to be acknowledged, and I am happy to do so. He might have exhibited more care,

237

though, in his characterization of my contribution. I focus on a reading of Freud that emphasizes the illuminating discussion of affect that is found throughout his aesthetic writings. I argue, further, that this discussion supplements what Freud says in his other writings about affect. My statement that ‘Freud needs to be updated’ is quoted out of context: it does not refer to what Rose terms ‘the negative psychoanalytic slant of art on emotion,’ but to my appreciation of Freud's interest in both artistic-cultural and scientific aspects of affect. Finally, Rose ignores the crux of my article: that the concept of ‘mentalized affectivity,’ a sophisticated form of affect regulation that entails the capacity to reflect on affective experience through the lens of one's representational history, incorporates a hermeneutic (drawn from art, culture and the clinical realm) and scientific (drawn from contemporary neuroscience) approach to the study of affect.

238

responseSet.title: Letters to The Editors: Reply to Nathan Szajnberg
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Letters to The Editors

Reply to Nathan Szajnberg

Siegfried Zepf

Siegfried Zepf

Narzissenstr 5, D-66119 Saarbrücken, Germany—s.zepf@rz.uni-saarland.de
10 January 2007

Siegfried Zepf  

Dear Sirs,

In replying to Szajnberg's comment I confine myself to five items.

Szajnberg leaves the reader in doubt as to whether he considers psychoanalysis to be a science or a belief. As he equates ‘correctness’ with ‘usefulness’, his criteria for a science are equally relevant for religious beliefs. Islam, for instance, is accurate, in its structure consistent with other beliefs, has a broad scope, is simple, is useful,

241

fruitful, and gives orders. Yet the crucial criterion of any science is not specifically mentioned, namely that its knowledge is—or at least attempts to be—in accord with reality. This is the criterion which distinguishes science from belief.

I agree with Szajnberg that one cannot take judgement on a theory's value by mere argumentation. However, he ignores the fact that—as Laudan (1977, p. 74) has pointed out—Kuhn fails to assess the decisive importance of conceptual problems in scientific research, and that conceptual clarity is vital in psychoanalytic research. If a theory is conceptually inconsistent—as psychoanalysis would be if it were infiltrated by attachment theory concepts—it can neither be useful nor can it inform psychoanalytic therapy or be empirically tested. Supposing there was not a consensual, conceptual definition of what is meant by ‘psychoanalytic process’, any empirical testing of the efficacy of psychoanalytic therapies would be impossible (see Gray, 2002).

Apparently, Szajnberg believes that observations are possible without any theory. Yet, prior to empirical investigation, any object of study has to be defined using categories. These categories belong to the theory used and connect the objects to be investigated in a specific manner. Otherwise we would not know what we have to look for. Or, borrowed in somewhat altered form from a famous statement of Kant: ‘Empirical investigation without theory is blind.’

Szajnberg simply trusts that, in clinical practice, some common ground for psychoanalysis and attachment theory can be found. Yet in the paper quoted, transference is not understood as a psychoanalytic issue but is explicitly operationalized within the framework of attachment theory (Szajnberg and Crittenden, 1997, p. 411).

responseSet.title: Reply to Michael Good
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Reply to Michael Good

Lawrence Brown

Lawrence Brown

37 Homer Street, Newton Centre, MA 02459, USA — larry.brown8@comcast.net
24 March 2007

Lawrence Brown  

Dear Editors,

Thank you to Michael Good for his careful reading of my paper and his thoughtful comments regarding the ‘analytic fate of screen memories’ in the psychoanalysis of traumatized patients. He is correct that I do not specifically address the presence of screen memories in the analysis of Julie which he views as a ‘case report [that] points toward … the fading of screen memories during a properly conducted analysis.’ I do not disagree with Good's assessment, which is quite possibly true from one perspective, but my emphasis was from a different vertex that involved the intersubjective play between the mind of the patient and analyst in creating meaning in statu nascendi.

Michael Good accurately states that ‘the analysis did not lead to a central focus on memories or reconstruction of sexual trauma.’ Julie initially lacked the capacity to think, dream and, therefore, historicize her traumatic experiences at the outset of our analytic venture. She was filled with ‘undigested facts’ (Bion, 1962) rather than memories, concretely experienced resonances that could not be thought about through secondary process thinking, but rather expelled through projective identification as an inchoate form of thinking. I hoped that Julie and I could become a ‘thinking couple’ (Bion, 1963) who could collaboratively process her inner world of frightening concrete presences through our interactive reveries. Julie was able through that exchange to have ‘memories’ and I was less concerned with the veridicality of these putative ‘memories’ to actual history than with the development of her ability to think.

Bion (1962; see also Ferro, 2002) distinguishes between the apparatus for thinking and the thoughts which that apparatus thinks, both of which undergo evolution that I believe was illustrated in my discussion of Julie's analysis. There was considerable growth in her capacity to think that evolved from a reliance on projec-tive identification to her ability to integrate her traumatic experiences in complex ways (historicize). Furthermore, there was an analogous growth in the nature of her thoughts from concrete β elements to α elements to intricate narratives. Screen memories, it seems to me, fall along this continuum.

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Consapevolezza ed autoanalisi [Awareness and self-analysis] edited by Pia De Silvestris and Adamo Vergine Milan: Angeli. 2005. 158 p.

Andrea Baldassarro

Andrea Baldassarro

Via Flaminia 48 - 00196 Roma, Italy — balthassar@katamail.com

Andrea Baldassarro  

In the unrelenting work to understand and transform the patient's psychic set-up, the analyst always moves on different planes, concerning his relationship with the patient and, in particular, his mental organization. If the activity of the mind can only be understood at a conscious and mindful level, it is the deep levels that are at stake in the constant effort to reveal the unconscious movements underlying the analysis.

This book is a comparative study of the different traditions of psychoanalysis in Italy, which has always been very interested in not only the deep dynamics of the relationship with the patient, but also the analyst's inner movements.

This is true for countertransference considered as a psychic movement following a specific treatment; it is especially true for the analyst's self-analysis. This is an ongoing inner process for the analyst, which is triggered and sustained by his analytical work and by the unexplored parts of his personal analysis. The fundamental issue presented in this study—that can be considered as an opportunity to stop and rethink within a broader framework of thoughts and considerations—is, the editors state, the possibility to ‘translate the subjective clinical experience into a shared experience’ (p. 7). It is an old issue, which is, however, always present in the psychoanalytical debate. Moreover, it is an issue that cannot be easily settled, even with a more sophisticated theoretical approach: there is still a wide gap—which may never be completely bridged—between clinical experience and theoretical generalization and formalization, notwithstanding the progress in the field of psychoanalytical research. In fact, it concerns the way in which the analyst thinks of and works through his own personal experience and therefore it refers to the origin of thought formation. According to the editors, by its very nature, psychoanalysis cannot be considered as a formalized science. However, the strength and originality of the analytical method result from the fact that it lies in a transitional area and it uses a peculiar way of thinking and of considering its own experience.

Psykoanalys i Välfärdsstaten—Profession, kris och framtid[Psychoanalysis in the welfare state—Profession, crisis and future] by Jürgen Reeder Stockholm/Stehag: Brutus Östlings Bokförlag Symposion.2006. 344 p.

Henning Paikin

Henning Paikin

Kildevænget 37, DK-2100 København Ø, Denmark — henning@paikin.dk

Henning Paikin  

This is a book which is in many ways unusual, interesting and informative, containing among many other things an analysis of the present crisis of psychoanalysis. And Reeder suggests a bold solution! The book may be seen as a continuation of and elaboration on themes from Reeder's (2004) examination of the profession's dilemma.

Reeder initially enumerates the reasons for the crisis: psychoanalysis has become financially inaccessible to people who pay for their treatment by taxed income; psychiatry—which has always felt estranged from psychoanalysis—has turned biological; psychoanalysis is increasingly in competition with newer psycho-therapeutic methods; at the same time, psychoanalysis has been subject to massive, predominantly unfounded attacks; and, finally, psychoanalysis has disappointed the exaggerated expectation that it alone would be able to solve all of the difficult tasks facing psychiatry.

Reeder sees psychoanalysis and psychoanalytical psychotherapy as a continuum with shared theory and clinical objectives. He believes it to be more pertinent to emphasize likenesses and shared interests than the differences. ‘As long as the clinical activity is guided by psychoanalytic knowledge, theory and objectives I would characterize it as “psychoanalytic.”’ He further states that he includes the large group of authorized psychotherapists in his discussion as they have common interests concerning the conditions for private practice (p. 13). He points out that the social authorities in Sweden, who authorize psychotherapists, use only the term ‘psychodynamic’—‘psychoanalytical’ is completely absent!

Chapter 1 consists of two parts: an ultra-short description of the history of psychiatry from c.

responseSet.title: Time driven: Metapsychology and the splitting of the drive by Adrian Johnston Evanston, IL: Northwestern University Press. 2005. 421 p.
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Time driven: Metapsychology and the splitting of the drive by Adrian Johnston Evanston, IL: Northwestern University Press. 2005. 421 p.

Britt-Marie Schiller

Britt-Marie Schiller

8820 Ladue Road, 3rd Floor, St. Louis, MO 63124, USA —schillbm@webster.edu

Britt-Marie Schiller  

The epigraph for this book is from Oscar Wilde: ‘In the world there are only two tragedies. One is not getting what one wants, and the other is getting it’. Of these, the latter is the more tragic. Oedipus gets what he wants, he does not renounce his instincts, so according to Freud's theory of civilization and its discontents, Oedipus should be a happy and non-neurotic man. This is, of course, not the case. The true tragedy is that the removal of inhibitions does not release the subject into enjoyment or jouissance. The fulfillment of unconscious desires is instead repulsive and terrifying. The foundational myth of psychoanalysis captures the uniquely human bind: desires are enjoyed only in so far as they are not realized.

The project here is to develop a metapsychological concept of drive (Trieb) that shows how the structural organization of drives inhibits our living out the drives and getting what we want. It is an ambitious project.

819

The legacy of tragedy. The tragic from Aristotle to our days edited by Andreas Giannakoulas and Sarantis Thanopulos Rome: Borla. 2006. 200 p.

Giovanna Goretti

Giovanna Goretti

Via Panama 114, I-00198 Roma, Italy — goretti.regazzoni@tiscalinet.it

Giovanna Goretti  

This is a little book which widely rewards the attention and the dedication demanded for its reading. It collects a selection of essays read on the occasion of the first international congress about ‘Tragedy then and today: From Aristotle to the third millennium’, which took place in 2002 in a place whose name is a legend— Stagira—and in a natural environment whose ‘sweet absence of any excess’—a quality which the world we are living in particularly needs—was underlined in the opening speech.

The little learned book has been edited by two psychoanalysts who, although Greeks by birth, have been Italians of adoption for many years: Andreas Giannakoulas and Sarantis Thanopulos, to whom we owe also many exacting translations of the published texts. To them go my keen thanks, but also the subtle remark for having pointed out to the congress the participation of exponents of art and international culture, but not always the specific disciplinary sphere which the authors of the texts in the volume belong to. The fact that psychoanalysts were present among the participants—and their texts are collected in a specific section of the volume—seems completely natural: to them Freud began to talk about the unconscious forces which govern the human being by means of the ‘tragic’ myth par excellence, the Oedipus myth.

Changing the order of the review, it is their texts (parochialism?) that I wish to begin to outline.

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

La sfida dell'irrapresentabile. La prospettiva ermeneutica nella psicoanalisi clinica [The challenge of the ‘un-representable’. The hermeneutic perspective in psychoanalysis] By Giuseppe Martini Milano: Angeli. 2005.

Alessandro Grispini

Alessandro Grispini

Via Archimende 179, I - 00198 Roma — al.gri@flashnet.it

Alessandro Grispini  

Giuseppe Martini, the author of this book, is a member of the Italian Psychoanalytic Society and lives in Rome, where he has a private practice and works as a psychiatrist for the social services. He is also the author of previous works on the role of hermeneutics in psychoanalysis. This book tackles the complex debate on the hermeneutic nature of psychoanalysis. On the one hand, it attempts to place

1Translated by Harriet Cooper.1311

the relationship between psychoanalysis and hermeneutics within the context of the great tradition created by the theorist Paul Ricoeur. On the other hand, it relaunches this relationship on new ground, in the realm of that which cannot be represented, a theme which would seem to have been designed to throw into question all the claims of hermeneutics.

Theoretical and clinical psychoanalysis have always had strong ties with the field of hermeneutics. The work of trying to transform the internal world of the patient through means of understanding and interpretation, which aims to uncover hidden meaning, to make sense of something and to make ‘thinkable’ that which has previously been ‘unthinkable’—this is ample justification for the case that psycho-analysis is a hermeneutic discipline.

The question of how to understand the relationship between psychoanalysis and hermeneutics has nevertheless generated a number of theoretical and clinical problems. If, on the one hand, hermeneutics has thrown into question the more naive forms of psychic determinism, on the other hand, it has produced theoretical developments, the most notable being the hermeneutic model which has emphasized the importance of narrative truth over and above historical truth. The impossibility of reconstructing a hypothetical historical truth (even one which is attainable), and moreover the impossibility of making truth a requirement for analytic interpretation, led to the identification of the essence of psychoanalysis—with its purely narrative function. Psychoanalysis aims to construct coherent stories—stories which are created jointly and shared by the analyst and the patient. The outcomes of the pure hermeneutic model are well known: meta-psychology is subjected to a radical critique, the fundamental aspects of the analytic relationship (asymmetry, neutrality, abstinence) are questioned in favour of a vision in which the boundaries between patient and analyst become meaningless (with an inevitable rethinking of the theory of transference and countertransference). Psychoanalysis and the unconscious lose their link with the dimension of biology and drives and the function of interpretation is often seen in terms of a kind of semiosis without limits.

responseSet.title: Further Thoughts on: Julie's Museum: The Evolution of Thinking, Dreaming and Historicization in the Treatment of Traumatized Patients
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Further Thoughts on: Julie's Museum: The Evolution of Thinking, Dreaming and Historicization in the Treatment of Traumatized Patients

Robert Withers

Robert Withers

The Rock Clinic, 270 Eastern Rd., Brighton, BN2 5TA — bob.withers@ntlworld.com
24 July 2007

Robert Withers  

Dear Editors,

I would like to comment on an assumption in Michael Good's (2007) recent letter on Julie's museum, one that with certain notable exceptions (e.g. Bohleber 2007) has remained largely unchallenged during the IJP's ongoing debate about the role of recovered memory in analysis (Fonagy, 1999, etc.). That assumption concerns the central importance to analysis of working in the transference/countertransference.

‘Screening’, writes Good, ‘can seductively divert the psychoanalytic endeavour from what is happening in the immediacy of the transference-counter transference and thereby constitute a defensive enactment, particularly when a particular reconstructive screen involving trauma is convincing to both patient and analyst’.

Leaving aside his conflation of Bion's beta screen with Freud's screen memory—which Lawrence Brown gently points out in his reply—what is Good actually saying here? Traumatic material arising in the course of an analysis, he seems to imply, should be distrusted as potentially seductive; while the sure ground of the transference/countertransference can be relied on. He goes on to reinforce this impression by suggesting that a diminishing emphasis on memory in an analysis may be an indicator of therapeutic progress.

Of course, it is possible to use memory defensively to evade uncomfortable feelings in the transference. But consider things for a moment from the perspective of a patient who is in the process of recovering a genuine traumatic memory. That memory might emerge first through a flashback, bodily symptom or post-traumatic nightmare in the form of β-elements that initially make little or no sense to either patient or analyst. Now if the analyst comments on the transference significance of these, in order to avoid being seduced by a screen, isn't the patient likely to conclude that this is because they are evoking unbearable feelings? In effect, the analyst is heard saying, ‘Let's not talk about your father raping you—let's talk about us’. The transference interpretation has now become a defensive enactment in the service of the resistance. This is likely to reinforce both the patient's dissociation and beta screen and may itself be a repetition of a denial of the original abuse. Even this repetition cannot be used therapeutically, however, because the emerging memory has been evaded and so remains in the form of unmetabalized β-elements.

Andres Arteaga and Linda Mayes

Linda Mayes

230 South Frontage Rd., New Haven, CT 06520 — linda.mayes@yale.edu

Linda Mayes  

In 1963, psychologist Sylvia Brody and her sociologist husband Sidney Axelrod began a ‘study of infant development’ that would go on to become one of the most ambitious longitudinal development studies based on parental interaction and psychoanalytic concepts. One-hundred-and-thirty-one families participated in parental/child interviews, observations, and completed batteries of psycho-educational and projective measures from birth through early childhood and beginning school years, and penultimately at 18 years. This book describes the 30 year follow-up of 76 of the original 131 participants.

Peter Schraivogel

Peter Schraivogel

Gartenstr. 26, Tübingen, 72074, Deutschland — peter.schraivogel@t-online.de

Peter Schraivogel  

How is external reality constructed in the individual's inner psychic world and how does his inner world in turn imprint the perception of external reality? This is a question with which psychoanalysts have always been concerned. To this day, we have found no satisfactory answer. Various psychoanalytic schools accord a different weight to external or inner reality in their understanding of clinical phenomena and sometimes they avoid looking with both eyes, as Scharff (2002) expressed it.

Many psychoanalysts deplore the inflationary way in which the concept of trauma is sometimes used. Mathias Hirsch poses this question at the outset of his book. He distinguishes between extreme traumatizations (concentration camps, wartime experiences, terror) that are more likely to lead to post-traumatic stress disorder and chronic familial traumatizations, which are considered to be the cause of severe personality disorders, often borderline pathologies (p. 3):

In one study, Dulz and Jensen (2000) found physical ill treatment and/or sexual abuse in 82% of the borderline patients who were being treated as in-patients; if this is extended to include cases of severe neglect, the figure rises to 100%. Whereas traumas in the rapprochement phase in very young children are more likely to relate to borderline disorders, narcissistic personality disorders are more often connected with deprivation traumas in babyhood. (p. 4)

Although this classification may sound rather schematic, it nevertheless suggests that we need to consider traumatizations in more detail.

Hirsch considers the concept of trauma to be a shorthand formula akin to ‘mother’, which must be subdivided into the traumatizing event, the traumatic state and the continuing pathological changes.

In addition to the immediate consequences, the overwhelming of the ego boundaries and the ego's integrational capacity, thus the overpowering anxiety, and the attempts to defend against it by dissociation and internalization, there are phenomena such as the loss of what is called primary trust (i.e. trust in the fundamental predictability and reliability of human relationships) and the weakening or destruction of the symbolizing capacity, along with the inadequate integration of the trauma into the explicit memory system. (p. 3)

[[RunningHead]]

Afternoon ‘Town Hall’ Meeting

Isaac Tylim

Isaac Tylim

128 Wooster St, New York, NY 10012, USA—ityork@aol.com

Isaac Tylim  

In his opening remarks to the three sections of this session, the moderator referred to Freud's writings in 1913 on the eve of World War I, on the interface of psychoanalysis and society and the important relationship between the individual and society. Dr. Tylim noted that Freud's contribution to aggression, the compulsion to repeat, and the return of the repressed may open new understanding to unresolved hatred and violence. He referred to ‘Why war?’, which argues ‘that community is held together by two things, the compelling force of violence and the identification between its members’ (Freud, 1933, p. 208). Love and work are the indicators of good mental health. Tolerance for differences is a valuable component of love.

[[RunningHead]]

Ipotesi Gay. Materiali per un confronto [The gay hypothesis. Material for comparative study] Edited by Olga Pozzi and Sarantis Thanopulos Roma: Borla. 2006. 219 p.

Manuela Fraire

Manuela Fraire

Via del Mascherone 60 — 00186 Roma, Italy — manuela.fraire@fastwebnet.it

Manuela Fraire  

This book is made up of contributions by five psychoanalysts (Giovanni De Renzis, author of the Preface, Fausto Petrella, Olga Pozzi, Sarantis Thanopulos, and Matilde Vigneri), by one molecular biologist (Riccardo Cortese), and by one historian of religion (Marcello Massenzio). As Pozzi, the co-editor of this volume with Thanopulos, writes, ‘Studying an issue such as homosexuality today as psychoanalysts cannot but confront us with the problematic question as to whether it is a normal or pathological phenomenon’ (p. 79).

What Freud has to say about sexuality in his Three essays (1905) remains an unavoidable starting point for anyone who wishes to discuss not only matters of sexual orientation, but also the dichotomies of nature vs. culture and instinct vs. learned behaviour. The authors, in their different ways, break away from the clichés which, following Freud, have characterized the ‘psychoanalysis of sexuality’, clichés which in recent decades have assumed a homophobic stance far removed from Freud's original intentions. The result is a book which does not attempt to come up with a single, homogenous and coherent theory of homosexuality, but rather seeks to convey the numerous perspectives on the subject. All of these, however, have in common a counterintuitive approach regarding the common-sense criteria of ‘normality’ in respect of homosexuality.

Among the most meaningful aspects of this approach (in De Renzis and Thanopulos's essays) are the questions raised about the traditional understanding of procreation as the (natural) primary aim of sexuality in the evolution of the species. The authors challenge the notion that human sexuality cannot exist independently of procreation and of the decision to have a family, although Petrella's position seems more ambiguous.

The authors support their anti-essentialist vision regarding sexual identity by observing the behaviour of animals, ranging from the fruit-fly (which features in De Renzis's Preface) to the primate (discussed in Cortese's essay). It is precisely in this approach that the authors distance themselves from Richard A. Isay, an openly gay analyst who believes that the foundations of homosexuality ‘are innate and the forms it takes are tied to the modalities of early relationships’ (p. 77).

[[RunningHead]]

L'enveloppe visuelle du Moi [The visual envelope of the ego] by Guy Lavallée Paris: Dunod. 1999. 266 p.

Denise Bouchet-Kervella

Denise Bouchet-Kervella

191 rue d'Alésia, F-75014 Paris, France—denise.bouchetkervella@wanadoo.fr

Denise Bouchet-Kervella  

This work is fairly representative of the main theoretical trends at work in contemporary French psychoanalysis. However, the authors to whom it makes most frequent reference, not only André Green and Didier Anzieu, but also Winnicott and Bion, are internationally well known. It constitutes a very dense conceptual whole that is nevertheless always rigorously argued and is interspersed throughout with clinical examples that help us to understand the complex trajectory of the author's thinking. The author proposes a truly psychoanalytic theory of visual perception, the product of an extensive study first outlined in Lavallée (1993), which is revised and completed here. This conception is based, and herein lies its originality, on the central idea that a hallucinatory dynamic that is simultaneously positive and negative lies at the heart of the functioning of the perceptual apparatus. According to the author, this apparatus is driven—constantly and from the outset of psychic life—by a hallucination of primal desire with two aspects. There is a positive aspect that gives rise to a proto-representation of the object in its absence, and a negative that provides protection from the surplus of the object's presence and constitutes a shield of Perseus that protects against the fear of being absorbed or pierced by the Gorgon's gaze.

The positive hallucinatory aspect tends towards a fusional continuity with the primary object, to binding and figuration, but must be modulated by the negative hallucinatory to allow perceptions to be libidinally cathected without then being confused with representations. Thus, although he is working directly in line with Anzieu's (1990) theory of psychic envelopes, Guy Lavallée diverges very markedly from this by introducing a general model of a negative hallucinatory envelope, a psychic structure emerging from complex drive impulses leading to the establishment, in relation to raw perceptions (Anzieu, 1990), of a filtering screen with three functions—protective shield, containing and reflexive—constituting a contact barrier that allows not only binding and differentiating between internal and external but also representing, understanding and symbolizing percepts.

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Psychotherapists as expert witnesses: Families at breaking point by Roger Kennedy London: Karnac. 2005. 235 p.

André Haynal

André Haynal

5 boulevard des Philosophes, CH-1205 Genève, Switzerland—andre.haynal@freesurf.ch

André Haynal  

This book presents a psychoanalytic approach from the point of view of an experienced senior expert witness of family dynamics and family law. The author sets out by ‘using psychoanalysis to understand the nature of the process involved with individuals, their relationships to each other and within a family context, as well as their interactions with the legal structures’ (p. 2). This implies making contact with

541

people in difficult circumstances. The author describes the independent attitude he takes in the difficult bridging of the identity of a psychotherapist-psychoanalyst and of an ‘expert’ in the realities of families in dramatic situations, i.e. ‘at breaking point’. He gives us a transparent description of what happens in these families and how we can help them.

In the first two chapters (up to p. 70), Kennedy gives an exact description of the procedures involved in the logical organization of his professional expert work and putting them into action. He discusses in detail the assessment of families, illustrated later by many gripping clinical examples. The cases are presented in an atmosphere of great understanding and empathy. The reader senses the author's patient listening skills, his attention to anxieties, defence, and subtleties of personality, as well as openness to the unknown. For instance, chapter 3 presents three insightful clinical examples to illustrate the most painful side of this kind of practice, namely the question of how to deal with the ‘best worst option’. The following chapter examines contact issues through a number of clinical examples including families affected by domestic violence. The penultimate chapter tackles questions of rehabilitation, adoption and fostering from a multi-disciplinary perspective. The last chapter, in addition to summarizing issues and conclusions, gives ideas about possible psychoanalytic contributions from the pertinent literature. Clearly, the book addresses a difficult task. One difficulty, among others, is the time dimension; the author comments insistently on the subjectively irritating ‘different time scales’ of the institutional and psychotherapy worlds (p. 36). Obviously this is a good example of many factors influencing countertransference. You hear a cry that ‘even the most experienced professional can occasionally come away from court feeling battered and bruised’ (p. 5).

Writing about patients. Responsibilities, risks, and ramifications by Judy Leopold Kantrowitz New York, NY: Other Press. 2006. 335 p.

Arnold Goldberg

Arnold Goldberg

122 S Michigan Ave, Suite 1305, Chicago, IL 60603, USA — docaig@aol.com

Arnold Goldberg  

Some books are fun to read. Some are important. Some have an immediate impact on the reader. Others have a lasting effect. It is to the great credit of Judy Kantrowitz that this book manages to do just about all of these.

The volume is a compendium of interviews that covers material ranging from describing just what analysts say and do when writing about patients, the effect of this writing on patients from the analyst's viewpoint, as well as patients' perceptions. These interviews are thorough and representative, and Kantrowitz manages to conduct an ongoing commentary and to serve as sort of a museum docent as we move from one section to the next.

803

responseSet.title: Adozione e oltre [Adoption and beyond] by Claudia Artoni Schlesinger Rome: Borla. 2006. 232 p.
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Adozione e oltre [Adoption and beyond] by Claudia Artoni Schlesinger Rome: Borla. 2006. 232 p.

Franco Borgogno

Franco Borgogno

Via Cavour, 46, I-10123 Torino, Italy — borgogno@psych.unito.it

Franco Borgogno  

As its title suggests, this book is not just about adoption; it also considers a wide range of psychological issues relating to the act of helping and protecting an ‘other’ mind, which has been displaced, at least temporarily, from ‘home’.

In speaking of adoption in this broader sense, we can say, like Ferenczi, that the process of adoption concerns the psychic pregnancy and then psychic birth which are necessary to allow the individual to accept the ‘other’ inside and outside of himself, so that he can recognize the other (in this case, the adopted child) in his difference and uniqueness. Therefore, this book is indeed about the adoptive functions, also within the psychoanalytic process, which are absolutely required in making contact with the ‘most primitive and often the most needy parts of the self’. These are the parts that did not receive the care and nurture they needed, when they needed it, and have not been able to develop. The subject that embodies these parts has remained unaware of them, has been unable to face them, or put them into words that would make it possible for him and others to understand them.

The author, a full member of the Italian Psychoanalytic Society, was trained as a psychoanalyst in Milan with a special group of ‘classmates’ (Dina Vallino Macciò, Antonino Ferro, Parthenope Bion Talamo, Stefano Bolognini and myself, to name but a few) who have gone on to become established internationally known psychoanalytical authors. During the course of her 30 years as a practising psychoanalyst, the author has had extensive experience of working in the field of adoption. She has worked with teams that select suitable couples from among those who, wishing to adopt, apply to the juvenile court in Milan. She has also worked as a psychoanalyst for adoptive mothers and adopted children at the Benedetta d'Intino Centre, Milan, a private clinic which provides diagnoses and psychoanalytic psychotherapy for babies, children and young people and for their families. But, to tell the truth, it is not the author's wide-ranging experience of this area which brings to life her unique account of adoption and its complex dynamics. The passion which runs through these pages derives from a very painful episode in the author's own life, which, once it had been worked through, brought her particularly close to the suffering of adopted children (see chapter 6, ‘Memories of my non-memories’, which is accompanied by a commentary by Dina Vallino Macciò).

Introductory volume: 144 p.; CD-catalogue: 1049 p. (Quellen und Abhandlungen zur Geschichte der Psychoanalyse [Sources and treatises on the history of psychoanalysis], Vol. 2. Schröter M, editor.)

Ernst Falzeder

Ernst Falzeder

Nr. 290, A-4582 Spital am Pyhrn, Austria—falzeder@pptv.at

Ernst Falzeder  

This bilingual compilation is published in collaboration with the Freud Museum, London.

As Gerhard Fichtner reminds us in his printed essay accompanying the CD catalogue, from early on Freud was a ‘book-worm’, whose ‘favourite food [was] books’ (pp. 10-1). Already during his school years, he found ‘infinite pleasure’ in ‘enlarging [his] beloved small library’ (p. 22), as he wrote to his friend Eduard Silberstein. To his fiancée he wrote in 1883 that ‘above all reading makes one independent and all one needs is perseverance, adventurousness and books’ (p. 22)—books, alas, that did not come for free, so that he also reported he had ‘bought some new trousers for 10 florins but books for 20 florins’ (p. 23).

Towards the end of his life, Freud's library was, as far as we know, ‘considerable but not huge’ (p. 15). It comprised about 3,600 titles (in some 4,500 volumes). Habent sua fata libelli [books have their fate]: When Freud emigrated to London in June 1938, he was able to take with him about two-thirds of his library, and gave the rest to a Viennese book-dealer, Paul Sonnenfeld, who in turn sold most of it to another dealer, Heinrich Hinterberger, while keeping 70 copies for himself. The collection in London remained more or less intact, and Anna Freud specified in her will that after her death the house and contents should become a Freud Museum, where the books are still housed today. Hinterberger's collection was sold as a whole to the Library of the New York State Psychiatric Institute, from which it later became a part of the Health Sciences Library of Columbia University.

549

responseSet.title: Reply to Dr Grotstein
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Reply to Dr Grotstein

Giovanna Goretti

Giovanna Goretti

Via Panama 114, 00198 Roma, Italy — goretti.regazzoni@libero.it
31 July 2007

Giovanna Goretti  

Dear Editors,

I am pleased that Dr Grotstein, an authority on projective identifi cation, appreciates my paper and is consonant with many of the points illustrated in it. I am particularly delighted that he has taken an interest in the many forms of identifi cation embraced by the concept of projective identifi cation as well as in the relation between projective identifi cation and knowing. Allow me to quote his description of the process of knowing, partly derived from Bion (1962) and partly from his own experience: ‘The subject—he writes—employs the projective identifi cation of inherent and acquired pre-conceptions on to the incoming stimuli emanating from the object. This is how we know reality’ (Grotstein, 2002). It seems to me that these words fit perfectly with empathy as well, as I have written in my text, and with the process of ‘becoming the patient’. Dr Grotstein does not explicitly say this, but I believe he also thinks that ‘becoming’ the patient must be a transient process that allows the analyst to return to being himself and not get into confused, inextricable situations.

1290

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Letters to The Editors

On: Art and Emotion in Psychoanalysis

Gilbert Rose

Gilbert Rose

P.O. Box 215, Rowayton, CT 06853, USA—giljrose@optonline.net
16 November 2006

Gilbert Rose  

Dear Sirs,

Jurist (2006) deplores the fact that psychoanalysis is not more aware of the positive role that art plays in emotion. He calls for a correction in the negative psychoanalytic slant of art on emotion. ‘Freud needs to be updated’ (p. 1330).

I would like to call to your attention to the fact that a body of published work long exists on precisely this subject (Rose, 1992, 1996a, 1996b, 2004a, 2004b, 2006, unpublished).

This continuing study of nonverbal aesthetic form in the light of psychoanalysis as well as neuroscience makes clear the mutual relevance of art and psychoanalysis. Affect serves as a biological taproot of art. Art contributes, in turn, to the ongoing developmental task of affect regulation and refinement.

A key theoretical issue is updating the longstanding mantra that secondary process succeeds primary process. Amending this in the light of art reveals that primary process forms interplay with secondary process discharge delay. This leads to an aesthetic experience of heightened awareness of feelingful thought and thoughtful feeling; this in turn enhances the sense of one's wholeness of self.

responseSet.title: A Farewell Message from the Editors
responseSet.authorMast: Glen O. Gabbard and  Paul Williams
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

A Farewell Message from the Editors

Glen Gabbard and Paul Williams

Paul Williams

Paul Williams  

With the publication of this issue, we end 5½ years as joint Editors-in-Chief of IJP. We pass the baton to Dana Birksted-Breen and Robert Michels, with confidence in their abilities to carry on a tradition started many years ago by Ernest Jones with the assistance of Sigmund Freud. When we took over as Editors in 2001, IJP entered a new era by selecting the first non-British editor (GOG). This joint editorship—a North American and a Brit working together—proved to be auspicious. Consultation with one another on difficult manuscripts was always valuable and often essential. We enjoyed each other's company and became fast and, subsequently, enduring friends. When one of us was indisposed for one reason or another, the other editor could keep things running smoothly. Perhaps the most important feature of this new arrangement was that it symbolized a principal theme of our tenure—namely, internationalization of IJP.

While IJP has always been the premier journal in the field, with the largest subscriber base and the most widely cited articles, various colleagues around the world had made it known to us that they sometimes questioned whether the publication was truly international. The internet had transformed the world into a global village, and psychoanalysis could not survive if it remained parochial. We decided to make sure that, under our tenure, IJP would reach out to as many analysts as possible, wherever they were. A cornerstone of this strategy was to engage more analysts from around the world as both reviewers and authors. We enlisted a ‘community of analysts’ to keep us abreast of developments in the analytic communities in every region of the globe.

In the service of internationalization, we enlisted the help of our Latin American regional editors, Elias Mallet da Rocha Barros with the capable help of Juan Francisco Jordán-Moore, and our European regional editor, Jean-Michel Quinodoz, and later, Antonino Ferro. With their able assistance, for which we are grateful, we found ways of taking the pulse of the readership to learn what was necessary to become a truly international publication. In addition to involving more reviewers and authors, we increased the number of peer-review workshops held at conferences around the world. These workshops involved debating the merits of a submitted paper, and they attracted large numbers of analysts who are interested in writing and publishing. We sponsored a major Latin American conference in Rio de Janeiro designed to explore specific themes within the psychoanalytic culture of Latin America. Many of the papers from this successful conference were subsequently published in IJP. In a similar vein, we sponsored a conference in Moscow that was enthusiastically received. One initiative that we hope will endure and develop is the introduction of IJP ‘annuals’ in various languages in which the best papers of the year are translated. Currently, French, Italian, German and Latin American analysts enjoy access to a wide range of international papers in their own language. A Russian annual is in preparation.

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Contribution to Correspondence on ‘Analytic Impasse and the Third’

Chris Milton

Chris Milton

268 M Albert Road, Sandringham, Auckland, New Zealand — chris.milton@xtra.co.nz
15 April 2007

Chris Milton  

Dear Editors,

I would like to make a small ‘ecumenical’ contribution to the correspondence following ‘Analytic impasse and the third’ (Aron, 2006, 2007; Stein, 2007). ‘Thirdness’ is an important (perhaps a central) notion in analytical psychology. ‘Thirdness’ appears in Psychological types (Jung, 1971) (originally published in German in 1921) which was itself something of a product of Jung's experience of the ‘impasse’ between Freud and himself. In this, Jung articulates how the ‘mediatory product’ of psychologically opposing perspectives (conscious and unconscious in that text) ‘becomes a new content … putting an end to the division and forcing the energy of the opposites into a common channel’ (p. 480).

1064

responseSet.title: Awakening the dreamer: Clinical journeys by Philip M. Bromberg Mahwah, NJ: The Analytic Press. 2006. 223 p.
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Awakening the dreamer: Clinical journeys by Philip M. Bromberg Mahwah, NJ: The Analytic Press. 2006. 223 p.

Melvin Lansky

Melvin Lansky

10921 Wilshire Blvd, Suite 509 Los Angeles, CA 90024 USA — mlansky@ucla.edu

Melvin Lansky  

Philip Bromberg's Awakening the dreamer is a collection of nine essays—an introductory chapter and eight revisions of previously published papers. The title is somewhat enigmatic: the book, except for its second and eighth chapters, is not primarily about dreams and dream interpretation as much as it is an exposition of Bromberg's version of relational psychoanalysis. However, the clinical primacy of bringing forward dissociated self states, typical of those of the dreamer behind the text of the dream narrative, orients us to the author's strong emphasis on self states, dissociation, collusion, enactment, and negotiation. The awakening of the dreamer stands, therefore, as the central organizing metaphor for the all-important task of working through of the propensity toward dissociation and thereby increasing the tolerance for the experience of intrapsychic conflict, which Bromberg, differing from the mainstream notion of conflict, understands to be the containing of more than one ‘conflicting’ self state in consciousness at the same time. Bromberg is a gifted clinical writer with a vivid style that conveys with immediacy the analytic encounter. His theoretical exposition is poetical, drawing liberally from literary sources and from his own autobiographical recollections.

Bromberg's book centers on the traumatized patient and privileges the immanence of the analytic encounter and the fullness of the analyst's, and, increasingly as the work unfolds, the traumatized and dissociation-prone patient's emotional participation. The felt lack of such fullness, both in the patient's first telling of the text of a remembered dream narrative and generally in the analytic session and in the patient's life, is, for Bromberg, a reflection of dissociation, not just in the analysand, but in the analyst as well, and it is reflected inevitably in the unfolding analytic process. It is in this context that Bromberg's central concepts come to bear: dissociation, that is to say, true hypnoid dissociation—he argues credibly that we have too soon dismissed Janet—results in the putting forward of one state of mind (self state) in such a way that it keeps other self states out of mind, in so far as these states or their accompanying affects are ‘incompatible’ with the currently dominant one. Dissociation in the patient inevitably affects the analyst in the analytic dyad and vice versa, and the analytic process unfolds with the collision of such dissociated self states in the one powerfully influencing the dominance of particular self states in the other. The result of such collision is enactment, the understanding and negotiation of which is the center of the analytic work. Any posited interpretive ‘reality’ that presumes to be about one person's psyche apart from the interactive, intersubjective flux partakes of the illusion that each of the two psyches is self-contained.

responseSet.title: Freud's Baby: Beyond Autoerotism and Narcissism
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Freud's Baby: Beyond Autoerotism and Narcissism

Dodd Cohen

Dodd Cohen

11500 W Olympic Blvd, Suite 625, Los Angeles, CA 90064, USA — doddcohen@aol.com (Final version accepted 18 September 2006)

Dodd Cohen  

autoerotism, narcissism, reality-ego, pleasure-ego, self-preservative instinct

The prevailing notion in the psychoanalytic literature is that Freud's thinking on neonatal object relations is completely captured in terms of his concepts of autoerotism and primary narcissism. Indeed, for Freud, autoerotism and primary narcissism conceptualize the earliest libido distributions, but these concepts do not exhaust Freud's model of early mental life. In this paper, the author endeavors to show that Freud's hypothetical infant arrives at autoerotism and narcissism at the expense of, and secondary to, primitive object-relatedness. More specifically, an appreciation of Freud's views on primitive object relations in light of the self-preservative instinct demonstrates his view that the infant is born into a state of mutual adaptation with the mother. The author makes detailed use of Freud's writings to show his conception of an infant who, from the inception of neonatal life, has the mental sophistication to maintain complex relations with instinctual objects, the sources of gratification or frustration, part-objects confusedly perceived because of cognitive immaturity and/ or fantasy distortion. Such complexity includes the infant's capacity for primitive forms of perception, boundary formation, reality testing, and defensive, splittingbased projections and introjections.

responseSet.title: Authors who have an Impact on Candidates' Training: Cultural Differences and Theoretical Languages
responseSet.authorMast: Andrea M. Rodríguez Quiroga De Pereira,  M. Rita Ragau,  Laura V. Borensztein De Weinstein and  Silvia G. Jadur
responseSet.authorCitation: De Pereira, A. M., Ragau, M. R., De Weinstein, L. V. & Jadur, S. G.
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Authors who have an Impact on Candidates' Training: Cultural Differences and Theoretical Languages11

Andrea De Pereira , M. Ragau , Laura De Weinstein and Silvia Jadur

Silvia Jadur

Bermúdez 306, La Lucila, 1636 Buenos Aires, Argentina — apereira@fibertel.com.ar

O'Higgins 1665, 1°B, 1426 Buenos Aires, Argentina — ragaurit@fibertel.com.ar

Fray Justo Sta, María de Oro 2779, 1º B, 1425 Buenos Aires, Argentina — lauraweinstein@fibertel.com.ar

Santa-Rosa 4952, 1414 Buenos Aires, Argentina — silviajadur@hotmail.com
(Final version accepted 12 October 2006)

Silvia Jadur  

candidate, scientific research, psychoanalytic training, international, culture, psychoanalytic theory

The authors consider the influence that a sense of geographical and cultural ties of candidates from different regions has on their theoretical interests. They question the way that this is taken into consideration in psychoanalytic training. The function of theory, both in terms of its transmission and the creation of new knowledge, is explored from this perspective. The results of an Internet survey are presented. The candidate sample for this survey (N = 250) was drawn from Europe, Latin America and North America, and candidates were asked to indicate their degree of interest for each of the 55 authors in a given list. The results showed that there were significant differences in the areas of theoretical interest of the candidates depending on the geographical region. Furthermore, what is also significant is how these differences in areas of theoretical interest were linked to those authors who had developed their work in the same geographical region as the candidates. These differences are shown to be connected to the candidates’ sense of regional belonging. Data are also presented about which authors have the greatest impact in a given region, along with the influence values of the authors in relation to each one of the regions. Finally, the candidates’ interest in each of the authors is specified in terms of a general mean rank and a regional mean rank, thus showing which authors candidates find most interesting in each of the regions. The study concludes by arguing that the results of the investigation enable us to question how psychoanalytic theory is transmitted, and, more specifically, how it is transmitted within institutions at a regional level. It is also suggested that the means be found to uncover the inconsistencies linked to cultural ties. It is proposed that further research be conducted to look more deeply into how cultural differences play a part in the different theoretical languages in the training of psychoanalysts.
