responseSet.title: Infant Observation at The Heart of Training By Janine Sternberg London: Karnac. 2005. 267 p.
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Infant Observation at The Heart of Training By Janine Sternberg London: Karnac. 2005. 267 p.

Lisa Miller

Lisa Miller

9 Bartholomew Villas, London, NW5 2LJ, UK—DDeFreitas@tavi-port.nhs.uk

Lisa Miller  

Not everybody, of course, thinks that infant observation is ‘at the heart of training’; at the heart of training, that is, in psychoanalysis or psychoanalytic psychotherapy. In child psychotherapy maybe; but not all other professionals in the field are sure of the relevance to their work of this rather particular discipline instituted by Esther Bick at the inception of the child psychotherapy training at the Tavistock Clinic in 1948. She was sure of the central part infant observation could—and in her view should—play in the training of psychoanalysts too, as she makes clear in Bick (1964), published 2 years after infant observation had been introduced into the curriculum of the Institute of Psychoanalysis, London. And the use of this discipline in a wide range of trainings has spread beyond London, so that the International Journal of Infant Observation and its Applications publishes 3 issues per year, and in 2006, was in volume 9.

However, it is not simply that some people remain unconvinced of the value of doing a baby observation: quite a lot of people do not know what it entails and what might be thought to be the benefits it brings. It is supposed, by those who have faith in it, to develop the intellectual and emotional muscle of the observer. The practice is this: every week, by careful prearrangement, the trainee visits a family at a regular time to observe an infant in context. There is usually at least one visit before the baby is born, and after the birth observations begin as soon as possible and go on until the child is 1-2 years old. The observer has to find a place in the family; friendly but neutral, he or she must discover by trial and error a way to watch the baby growing up in its family setting. It can be hard to avoid the dangers of being overfriendly on one hand or chilly on the other. There is a good deal of anxiety to be tolerated here, as observers remember that they are there by the courtesy of the families. It is up to them to remind themselves that they are there to learn, and their subject is this unique child. Each observation lasts an hour. Afterwards, the observer leaves to write up a detailed account of the observation before it has faded. These process notes are discussed in a small seminar group where the anonymity of the family is preserved and the bounds of confidentiality kept.

Richard Waugaman

Richard Waugaman

4701 Willard Ave. #204, Chevy Chase, MD 20815, USA—rwmd@comcast.net

Richard Waugaman  

The Portuguese author Fernando Pessoa has been called ‘the greatest twentieth-century writer you have never heard of’ (Zenith, 2006, outside back cover). Remarkably, he published poetry under three major pseudonyms (and had some 70 others). Pessoa was deeply influenced by Walt Whitman, who once wrote,

Do I contradict myself?

Very well then I contradict myself,

I am large, I contain a multitude.

(Whitman, 1993, p. 76)

Pessoa called his alter egos ‘heteronyms.’ He considered them full-fledged individual writers, whose work he sometimes strongly disliked, and who interacted (and conflicted) with one another.

267

responseSet.documentRef: Cohen, S. (2007) Mother in psychoanalysis: A feminist view [in Hebrew] by Anat Palgi-Hecker Tel Aviv: Am Oved. 2005 358 p.. International Journal of Psychoanalysis  88:272-276
responseSet.documentRefHTML: Cohen, S. (2007) Mother in psychoanalysis: A feminist view [in Hebrew] by Anat Palgi-Hecker Tel Aviv: Am Oved. 2005 358 p.. International Journal of Psychoanalysis 88:272-276

responseSet.documentInfoXML: 2007 88 1 272-276 Mother in psychoanalysis: A feminist view [in Hebrew] by Anat Palgi-Hecker Tel Aviv: Am Oved. 2005 358 p. Shlomith Cohen 6 Yoav St, Jerusalem 93151, Israel—shcohenb@gmail.com
responseSet.title: Mother in psychoanalysis: A feminist view [in Hebrew] by Anat Palgi-Hecker Tel Aviv: Am Oved. 2005 358 p.
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Mother in psychoanalysis: A feminist view [in Hebrew] by Anat Palgi-Hecker Tel Aviv: Am Oved. 2005358 p.

Shlomith Cohen

Shlomith Cohen

6 Yoav St, Jerusalem 93151, Israel—shcohenb@gmail.com

Shlomith Cohen  

Anat Palgi-Hecker has embarked on a venture to portray the mother in psychoanalytic thinking from a feminist perspective. Her main thesis asserts a place for the mother's subjective experience. She claims that, although the mother has become increasingly important in developmental psychoanalytic theory and in understanding object relations in the last decades, the mother's own subjective existence has been left in the dark. Equipped with feminist cultural criticism and sensitivity, Palgi-Hecker rides the deep waters of the most classical psychoanalytic thinkers to look for the mother and try to understand her absence as a protagonist in most psychoanalytic accounts of the human experience.

This book emerged from the author's doctoral thesis for Haifa University, Israel, and enquires into an impressive body of psychoanalytic texts, the biographies of their authors and their relationships with other main figures of their time. The extensive and varied bibliography, thoroughly studied and discussed, shows the author's erudition and is an invaluable source for further study and research in this field.

Palgi-Hecker's research was not motivated by mere intellectual zest. Rather, she uses her feminist sensitivity to speak in a full, clear voice against the view that ignores the mother as a subject. The author's expedition goes beyond uncovering an unknown continent and is designed to emancipate the mother from the confinement of the paternal and patriarchal grip. Throughout her work she refrains from a neutral stance and asserts a strong, ethical commitment to the feminist position that strives to define the mother as an experiencing subject.

The feminist perspective on motherhood poses several intriguing questions:

Where is motherhood located between an essentialist view that emphasizes the biological capacity to bear a child and the constructivist view of the mother as a psycho-social construct?

To what extent is there an inherent psychoanalytic connection between female sexuality and motherhood?

responseSet.title: The body as mirror of the world by Janine Chasseguet-Smirgel Leighton S, translator. London: Free Association Books. 2005 165 p.
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

The body as mirror of the world by Janine Chasseguet-Smirgel Leighton S, translator. London: Free Association Books. 2005165 p.

Jeanne Bernstein

Jeanne Bernstein

1808 4th St, Berkeley, CA 94710, USA—jwbernstein@msn.com

Jeanne Bernstein  

This book, a translation of Chasseguet-Smirgel (2003, Le corps comme miroir du monde), has sadly become the author's last. With her unexpected death in early March 2006, Chasseguet-Smirgel has left us with a powerful manifesto in which she addresses an issue that has been dear to her all of her life—the body-mind dualism in all its manifestations and deformations. The book is divided into two parts: the first, written after September 11th, 2001 (9/11); and the second, written before. This non-chronological order is confusing at times and somewhat stalls the flow. Yet, at the risk of backshadowing too much, I believe that Chasseguet-Smirgel chose this path because she may have experienced a sense of urgency to publish her last reflections about a world in which human beings are constantly held in suspense and frightened by ever-increasing violent attacks on and annihilations of the body.

Shocked by the 9/11 terrorist attacks, Chasseguet-Smirgel postulates that everyone must wonder about the constitution of a human being who so readily sacrifices his own body to destroy those of others. Although the history of religion is filled with stories of bodily self-sacrifice, such as Jesus Christ or St Catherine, the destruction of one's own body to kill others seems to be a unique 20th century phenomenon where the body itself is transformed into a killing machine. Although Chasseguet-Smirgel does not say so directly, she nonetheless implicitly argues that multiple trends in the cultural conscious and unconscious already existed, which prepared the way for the misuse of the body in such a horrific manner as shown by the suicide bombers. When read this way, the non-chronological order of her book makes more sense because the first part, ‘Reaching the light,’ then reads like the manifest content of her project, while the second, ‘The rebellion against the biological order,’ becomes the latent content.

To prepare her readers for her argument that the current political violence has grown out of many literary, cinematic, and historical currents in which the destruction of the body is celebrated in one form or another, Chasseguet-Smirgel chooses Foucault, Pasolini, and the Japanese writer Mishima as her ‘constant companions.’ Beginning with the emblematic description by Foucault from The order of things (1970) of a French prisoner whose already mutilated body is torn into quarters by six different horses, Chasseguet-Smirgel launches into a discussion of literary and cinematic works of art where the mutilated body is worshipped as an exciting and voyeuristic spectacle. With particular attention to Confessions of a mask (1960), The sailor who fell from grace with the sea (1966), and Sun and steel (1971)

responseSet.title: Freud by Jonathan Lear New York, NY: Routledge. (Routledge Philosophers series.) 2005. 278 p.
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Freud by Jonathan Lear New York, NY: Routledge. (Routledge Philosophers series.) 2005. 278 p.

Marcia Cavell

Marcia Cavell

375 Riverside Drive, 9B, New York, NY 10025, USA—mcavell@earthlink.net

Marcia Cavell  

Jonathan Lear is one of the most subtle and original thinkers in psychoanalysis. So a book by him simply called Freud should attract everyone who is at all psycho analytically minded. They will not be disappointed. This is simply the best introduction to Freud I know, and perhaps illuminating particularly for those who are clinically practiced.

How should I live my life? Lear poses the question at the beginning of this book, and it sets the tone for the rest. Lear sees the psychoanalytic conversation as a moral discourse in the widest sense. In a late chapter in the book, he shows that what Freud calls the reality and pleasure principles are basically ethical in nature. Heeding the reality principle is indispensable to integrity, and it is precisely this which neurosis threatens. The reality principle is linked to virtues in the classic aristotelian sense: habits, or recurrent ways of being, that allow for human flourishing. Neurotic symptoms are also habits, recurrent ways of being, that on the other hand inhibit human flourishing. Lear coins the word ‘neurtue’ to name ‘a psychologically organized orientation that entirely distorts [one's] ability to understand [her] world, and [her] place in it’ (p. 153).

Neurosis, in Lear's view, and, he persuasively argues, in Freud's as well, makes inroads on moral agency, and undercuts one's ability to function as a responsible agent. I think this is right. But I see one difficulty which a moral framing of neurosis might pose for the analyst: it would be easy for her to slip from a moral view to a moralistic

532

[[RunningHead]]

Die vergessene Kunst: Der Orpheusmythos und die Psychoanalyse der Musik [Forgotten art: The Orpheus myth and the psychoanalysis of music] by Sebastian Leikert Giessen: Psychosozial-Verlag. 2005. 175 p.

Josef Dantlgraber

Josef Dantlgraber

7 Neckargasse, D-72070 Tübingen, Germany—Josef.Dantlgraber@web.de

Josef Dantlgraber  

This book is to my knowledge the first monograph concerning the relationship between psychoanalysis and music; it sets out to define the essential nature of music using psychoanalytic concepts. The book consists of essays written by the author in the last few years, some of which have been published in renowned journals. There is nevertheless a unity to it because the texts both build on and complement each other so as to enable us to reconstruct the way in which Leikert's thoughts on this subject have developed.

1Translated by Sophie Leighton.538

responseSet.title: On: Julie's Museum: The Evolution of Thinking, Dreaming and Historicization in the Treatment of Traumatized Patients
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

On: Julie's Museum: The Evolution of Thinking, Dreaming and Historicization in the Treatment of Traumatized Patients

Michael Good

Michael Good

74 Craftsland Road, Chestnut Hill, MA 02467, USA — michaeligood@pol.net
12 February 2007

Michael Good  

Dear Editors,

In the subtext of a lucid paper on psychic trauma and the narrativization of the analysand's past and present experience through the exploration of the transference- countertransference, Lawrence J. Brown (2006) indirectly gives evidence of an important phenomenon involving screen memory and screen trauma that is particularly relevant to our understanding of early traumatic memories and their possible evolution over the course of psychoanalysis. The case report points toward (but does not explicitly identify) the fading of screen memories during a properly conducted analysis. The lessening or lifting of screens has clinical and scientific implications for psychoanalysis, especially regarding the controversial current status of the seduction hypothesis (Good, 2006). Illustrations of the vicissitudes of screens, however, are not so well documented in the analytic literature.

Brown notes that, ‘The analyst's [containing and transforming reveries] … assist the birth in the patient of increasingly sophisticated ways of giving meaning to the traumatic episodes in her life’ such that, in successful treatments, ‘the patient's capacity to represent the trauma evolves from the concrete to the progressively more abstract, culminating in the historicization of the previously unthinkable [and unsymbolized] experiences’ (p. 1582). His sensitive, careful, and self-reflective approach with the patient helped her accomplish this creative and therapeutic task. Yet it does not always work out that way. Instead, as the patient's initially reported history of sexual abuse suggests, the ‘β-screen’ may predispose to screen memory and screen trauma, and these at times lead analyst and patient astray (Good, 1994, 1998). Screening can seductively divert the psychoanalytic endeavor away from what is happening in the immediacy of the transference-countertransference and thereby constitute a defensive enactment, particularly when a particular reconstructive screen involving trauma is convincing to both patient and analyst. Screens can have a greater or lesser degree of veridicality about what once actually happened to a patient. Screen memories of early traumatic experience are probably a rather common occurrence and involve the tendency to condense, displace, or, as Brown notes, concretize early experience.

responseSet.title: Reply to Paolo Migone
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Reply to Paolo Migone

Alan Sugarman PHD

Alan Sugarman

744 Munevar Rd, 92007 Cardiff By The Sea CA, USA — mumford2@cox.net
25 February 2007

Alan Sugarman  

Dear Editors,

I would like to thank Paolo Migone for drawing my seeming neglect of Kris's (1956) ‘good analytic hour’ article to my attention. I, myself, was surprised at this apparent omission as I had referenced it in my earlier article applying these ideas to child analysis (Sugarman, 2003). But rereading Kris left me believing I should have included the article because of its importance in the literature, not because it foreshadowed my own thinking. Nowhere in the article could I find evidence supporting Migone's assertion that Kris ‘explicitly advocated a change of the concept of insight from “content” to “process”.’ To be sure, such ideas may be implicit but I could not discern them. My difficulty doing so might involve Kris's use of the ego-psychological model. Elsewhere (Sugarman, in press), I have discussed my preference for more clinically-near structural concepts such as mental functions over abstract theoretical constructs like the tri-partite mental structures. Kris's emphasis on the relationship between the three structures and the ego's integrative function, in particular, makes a direct and explicit comparison of our ideas difficult.

Given this caveat, Kris seemingly was interested in a related but somewhat different aspect of insight.

773

responseSet.title: The Return of the Erased: Memory and Forgetfulness in Eternal Sunshine of the Spotless mind (2004)
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

The Return of the Erased: Memory and Forgetfulness in Eternal Sunshine of the Spotless mind (2004)

Michel Gondry and Havi Carel

Havi Carel

Havi Carel, Faculty of Humanities, Languages and Social Sciences, University of the West of England, St Matthias Campus, Oldbury Court Road, Bristol, BS16 2JP, UK —
havi.carel@uwe.ac.uk

Havi Carel  

Freud, mourning, melancholia, death, ambivalence, identification, Klein, loss

Eternal sunshine of the spotless mind is a film about remembering and forgetting loss. This essay reads the film as an examination of mourning and melancholia, which are distinct ways of remembering and forgetting both a love object and its loss. Freud distinguished mourning from its pathological counterpart, melancholia, claiming that there is a normal way to grieve, mourning, and its degeneration into an abnormal pattern, melancholia. The author aims to make two points: firstly, that both processes are characterized by ambivalence and identification and therefore have some commonalities; secondly, that there is a difference between the two processes that is less apparent than the ones discussed by Freud. This is the difference between remembering a good and a bad object. Following Klein the author argues that this is a crucial qualitative difference between mourning and melancholia. She concludes that a central issue in grieving is not forgetting but remembering well.

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

La adopción: Un tema de Nuestro Tiempo[Adoption: A Contemporary Topic] edited by Milagros Cid edited by Silvia Pérez-Galdós Madrid: Biblioteca Nueva.2006.222 p.

María Angeles Albamonte

María Angeles Albamonte

Bachiller, 32, E-46010 Valencia, Spain — angelesalbamonte@hotmail.com

María Angeles Albamonte  

This book collects together the papers presented at the ‘Adoption of children and adolescents’ lecture series organized by the Centro de Atención Clínica e Investigación [Center for Clinical Practice and Research] in October 2000, whose speakers were members of the Department of Child and Adolescent Psychoanalysis of the Asociación Psicoanalítica de Madrid [Madrid Psychoanalytic Association]. The book has, therefore, many contributors, who have chosen different perspectives from which to analyze a topic that is very much present, not only in the media and in our practice, but especially in the life of many families who choose this form of parenthood for a variety of reasons.

This is a diverse and complex text that evinces the extensive experience of all the authors and clearly shows that psychoanalytic theory is their common foundation. Throughout each lecture, all the spaces and borders of adoption gradually find their place—institutions, professionals working there or in private practice, parents of diverse personal backgrounds who want to adopt and do so with very different motivations, and the relationships established among institutions, parents, and children who, being adopted at different ages, bear an array of internal histories.

Psychoanalytic theory throws light upon the vicissitudes of adoption, which is a very complex human experience. Two foreign parties meet at a certain moment, and from then on they will become adopting parents and adopted children. Both parties have to carry out specific tasks to ensure that the filiation experience leads to the construction of a family. Among such tasks, mourning processes play the main role. There are those undergone by each party, and those specific to the encounter, even though the latter may be either tinged with hope and love or concealed by denial and omnipotence.

Many psychoanalytic concepts traverse the book: extreme infantile dependence and the Other's structuring role; the significance of early object relations in their satisfying and/or traumatic aspects; the internal world, which will gradually acquire structure through the interchanges of one's own drives and the other human being; separation anxiety; processes of identification; the Oedipus complex; the family romance; the structuring of sexual identity, and so many others that render this book a valuable text for those familiar with psychoanalytic thought and having interest in this topic. In this way, this collection differs from many mass-market books intended for parents who want to better understand the disconcerting situations they sometimes face when the reality of their contact with their adopted children does not correspond to their dreams.

responseSet.title: On: Projective Identification
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

On: Projective Identification

James Grotstein

James Grotstein

522 Dalehurst Avenue, Los Angeles, CA 90024-2516, USA — jgrotstein@earthlink.net
5 June 2007

James Grotstein  

Dear Editors,

I should like to compliment Dr. Giovanni Goretti (2007) on her admirable and scholarly review of the concept of projective identification and her excellent enumeration of its various uses. She has studiously surveyed the vast landscape of this entity. Her grasp of the concept is noteworthy and includes a span of its normal and pathological implications as well as the genesis of its early development in the works of Klein, Rosenfeld, Bion, and others. She asked two major questions: is the concept too broad and over-inclusive as it now stands, and ‘to what extent is it possible to know reality without having recourse to projective identification’? (p. 403, original italics). I should like to suggest a brief reply to both questions.

In my own work on the subject, I suggested that the definition of projective identification should conform to Klein's original conception as an intra-psychic omnipotent phantasy involving part-objects, whereas I propose the term ‘projective trans identification’ for its intersubjective (transpersonal, whole object) manifestations (Grotstein, 2005). As an unconscious phantasy it subserves the mechanism of intra-psychic displacement of part-objects in unconscious phantasy (i.e. makebelieve), whereas in its whole object function some additional mechanism must be added for it to subserve unconscious communication between two real human individuals (whole objects). In that contribution I hypothesized that, even though one can speak about projecting into another person experientially or in unconscious phantasy, one cannot project in actual reality into another person metapsychologically, only into one's image (internal object) or representation of that person—and then seek to manipulate that image (part-object) via ‘sympathetic magic’ (confusing the whole object with the part-object) to control the whole object magically. When the object actually does respond, an additional factor may be at work, gesturing, nudging, or priming. Bion (1973, personal communication, 1978)

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Psykoanalysen i ny belysning [Psychoanalysis in a new light] by Gunnar Karlsson Stockholm: Symposion. 2004. 229 p.

Søren Aagaard

Søren Aagaard

Tolstojs Alle 43B DK 2860 Søborg — s.aagaard@get2net.dk

Søren Aagaard  

Gunnar Karlsson formulates his project in a chapter on the psychoanalytic conceptualization of the psyche:

The psychoanalytic process begins with the analysand's conscious self-understanding and is driven forward by means of conscious validations of interpretations of the unconscious. Our knowledge of the unconscious is always based on the conscious (contents of consciousness) … that is why, to understand the unconscious, we first have to understand what specifically characterizes consciousness. In the discussion of what distinguishes consciousness I shall make reference to phenomenology. (p. 81)

The writer aims at bringing psychoanalysis and phenomenology together with the purpose of making his contribution that psychoanalysis may become founded on a more adequate theory of knowledge, that is a meta-theory where the abstract and theoretical constructions of human sciences can be ‘justified through their foundation in the life-world (Lebenswelt) of the subject’ (p. 35). Husserl (1952) is quoted: ‘Phenomenology is in our opinion the source of science, the mother of all thinking’ (p. 35).

The first part of the book is a philosophical and epistemological exposition of Husserl's phenomenology: the teachings about the natural attitude that characterizes everyday experiences and the teachings on the phenomenological reductions where the question: ‘How is it possible that I experience (something)?’ forces us to reflect on the experience itself (experiencing); Karlsson points out that this is an ontological problem. Consciousness is always consciousness about something and is essentially intentional. Intentionality gives direction and meaning. The concept of meaning is at the very core of phenomenology: the (subjectively) experienced meaning always relates to an experience and to a way of experiencing. The author finds a number of inter-connections between psychoanalysis and phenomenology: the interest in the subjective, in intentionality and meaning, in the importance of reflection and in responsibility as an ethical principle.

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Recognizing the Infant as Subject in Infant-Parent Psychotherapy

Frances Salo

Frances Salo

PO Box 2226, Caulfield Junction, Melbourne, VIC 3161, Australia — fvtsalo@unimelb.edu.au
(Final version accepted 21 September 2006

Frances Salo  

failure to thrive, infant as subject, infant-parent psychotherapy, mirror neurons, reciprocal interactive intervention, therapeutic action

Drawing on Winnicott's view of infants as subjects entitled to an intervention in their own right, infants as the referred patient have been seen in infant-parent psychotherapy for 20 years at the Royal Children's Hospital, Melbourne, Australia. This is a radically different view of infant symptomatology than viewing it as only expressing an aspect of the mother's unconscious. The clinical pathway differentiates the therapy from much parent-infant psychotherapy. The author describes the theoretical model of a twofold approach to understanding the infant's experience through interactive dialogue between therapist and infant, and sharing this understanding with the parents, and illustrates it with cases of failure-to-thrive infants. She discusses two criticisms: first, that infant-parent psychotherapy may undermine the parents and, second, that brief parent-infant psychotherapy does not alter parents’ insecure attachment status. Videotaped sessions often show rapid improvement; parents generally feel relieved. This approach potentially shapes not only parents’ and infants’ representations, but also their implicit knowledge of relationships—partly, it is suggested, through activating the mirror neuron system to bring about implicit memory change. Change may therefore be longer lasting than psychoanalytic theory presently conceives. The approach is relevant in an outpatient setting: gains were maintained long term in 90% of out-patient cases.

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Psychoanalytic Transformations11

Fernando Riolo

Fernando Riolo

Italy — frnriolo@tin.it
(Final version accepted 21 January 2007)

Fernando Riolo  

rigid motion transformation, projective transformation, hyperbolic transformation, transformation in hallucinosis, preconception, predetermination

The author describes how Bion took Freud's conception of dreams as a form of thought and used it as the basis of his theory of transformations. Bion developed an expanded theory of ‘dream thought’, understood as a process of selection and transformation of sensory and emotional experiences. In this theory, the work of analysis is in turn conceived as a process not only of deciphering symbols, of revealing already existing unconscious meanings, but also of symbol production— of a process for generating thoughts and conferring meaning on experiences that have never been conscious and never been repressed because they have never been ‘thought’. Analysis, in its specific operational sense, becomes a system of transformation whereby unconscious somatopsychic processes acquire the conditions for representability and become capable of translation into thoughts, words and interpretations. The rules of transformation applied by the patient in his representations and those applied by the analyst in his interpretations have the same importance for the analytic process as those described by Freud for the process of dreaming. The author discusses the broad categories of transformation adduced by Bion (rigid motion, projective, and in hallucinosis) and introduces some further distinctions within them.

responseSet.title: Sibling Loss, Guilt and Reparation: A Case Study
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Sibling Loss, Guilt and Reparation: A Case Study

Christopher Christian

Christopher Christian

60 West Olsen Road, #4200, Thousand Oaks, CA 91360, USA—cctian@earthlink.net
(Final version accepted 8 April 2006)

Christopher Christian  

sibling loss, sibling rivalry, negative therapeutic reaction, loss

Psychoanalysis has shown that the death of a sibling is likely to have a long-standing impact on the character development of a surviving child. Among common adult manifestations are the identification with the deceased sibling, repetitive self-punitive behaviors, and the development of masochistic trends. In treatment, these patients can become entrenched in a negative therapeutic reaction that compromises the outcome of their analysis. In this paper, the author discusses the analysis of a woman with a history of losses that included the loss of a sibling at an early age. A critical part of the treatment focused on helping this patient overcome a negative therapeutic reaction that emerged as she became aware of hostile and vengeful fantasies, not only as they related to her deceased brother but, more importantly, as they related to her parents.

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

The Intersubjective Links in Perversion11

Alberto Eiguer

Alberto Eiguer

154 rue d'Alesia, F-75014 Paris France — albertoeiguer@voila.fr (Final version accepted 19 October 2006)

Alberto Eiguer  

intersubjective link, pervert/victim—accomplice link, pervert/analyst link, predisposition, force of attraction, domination, formation of groups, witness

The author studies the intersubjective links which the pervert maintains with analyst or partner, attempting to indicate the differences between the investments in each case. Rather than accepting that empathy towards these patients is impossible to achieve and disturbs the countertransference profoundly, it attempts to show that these difficulties may be overcome if they are reinterpreted in the light of the theory of the intersubjective link. The author examines the theories and the practice of intersubjectivity and gives a definition of his approach to the link between two subjects. He applies these ideas to the case of a sexually masochistic female patient. The countertransference is marked successively by indifference, rejection and smothering. The analysis of the analyst's dream allows the situation to evolve. Failures in primary identification can result in domination over others and utilitarianism. The author examines the place of the challenge to the ‘Law’ and the father (in the attempt by the patient to put a theory to the test) in order to identify the figure of the witness in the pervert's intersubjective links. The desire of the transference would be marked by the figure of the witness rather than by that of the analyst as accomplice.

responseSet.title: Elements of Analytic Style: Bion's Clinical Seminars
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Elements of Analytic Style: Bion's Clinical Seminars

Thomas Ogden

Thomas Ogden

306 Laurel Street, San Francisco, CA 94118
(Final version accepted 6 December 2006)

Thomas Ogden  

style, technique, dreaming up a patient, psychosis, Bion

The author finds that the idea of analytic style better describes significant aspects of the way he practices psychoanalysis than does the notion of analytic technique. The latter is comprised to a large extent of principles of practice developed by previous generations of analysts. By contrast, the concept of analytic style, though it presupposes the analyst's thorough knowledge of analytic theory and technique, emphasizes (1) the analyst's use of his unique personality as reflected in his individual ways of thinking, listening, and speaking, his own particular use of metaphor, humor, irony, and so on; (2) the analyst's drawing on his personal experience, for example, as an analyst, an analysand, a parent, a child, a spouse, a teacher, and a student; (3) the analyst's capacity to think in a way that draws on, but is independent of, the ideas of his colleagues, his teachers, his analyst, and his analytic ancestors; and (4) the responsibility of the analyst to invent psychoanalysis freshly for each patient. Close readings of three of Bion's ‘Clinical seminars’ are presented in order to articulate some of the elements of Bion's analytic style. Bion's style is not presented as a model for others to emulate or, worse yet, imitate; rather, it is described in an effort to help the reader consider from a different vantage point (provided by the concept of analytic style) the way in which he, the reader, practices psychoanalysis.

responseSet.title: Remembrance, Trauma and Collective Memory: The Battle for Memory in Psychoanalysis1
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Remembrance, Trauma and Collective Memory

The Battle for Memory in Psychoanalysis1

Werner Bohleber

Werner Bohleber

Kettenhofweg 62, D-60325 Frankfurt am Main, Germany—wbohleber@gmx.de

Werner Bohleber  

remembrance, memory, trauma, reconstruction, collective memory, Holocaust, generation

In contemporary clinical theory in psychoanalysis, remembering life-historical events and reconstructing the past have lost the central therapeutic function that they had for Freud. The author describes this development and demonstrates the way in which trauma and its remembrance resist it. He discusses the problem of the truth status of memories. Traumatic memories are not subject to transformation by the present when they are retrieved. They constitute a kind of foreign body in the psychic-associative network, but rather than forming an exact replica of the traumatic experience they undergo specific remodellings. The author describes some of the psychic processes in this encapsulated realm. Resolving its predominant dynamics and extricating phantasy from traumatic reality require a remembrance and reconstruction of the traumatic events in the analytic treatment. The author goes on to describe the vital importance of social discourse concerning historical truth for both the individual concerned and society in connection with disasters defined as man-made. A reluctance to know often sets in here that stems from the desire to avoid confronting the crimes, the horror and the victims' suffering. With the Holocaust in particular, the further problem arises of how to avoid its subjugation in historical description to defining categories that eliminate the horror and traumatic nature of the events. Remembering crimes unfolds a special set of dynamics. The author describes both these dynamics and their transgenerational effects on post-war German society. He concludes that, in order to confront the problems posed by a multifaceted traumatic reality, it is also necessary to battle to restore memory to an appropriate place in psychoanalysis.

responseSet.title: On talking-as-dreaming
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

On talking-as-dreaming

Thomas Ogden

Thomas Ogden

306 Laurel Street, San Francisco, CA 94118, USA
(Final version accepted 9 April 2006)

Thomas Ogden  

talking, dreaming, reverie, waking-dreaming, undreamable experience, undreamt dreams

Many patients are unable to engage in waking-dreaming in the analytic setting in the form of free association or in any other form. The author has found that ‘talking-as-dreaming’ has served as a form of waking-dreaming in which such patients have been able to begin to dream formerly undreamable experience. Such talking is a loosely structured form of conversation between patient and analyst that is often marked by primary process thinking and apparent non sequiturs. Talking-as-dreaming superficially appears to be ‘unanalytic’ in that it may seem to consist ‘merely’ of talking about such topics as books, films, etymology, baseball, the taste of chocolate, the structure of light, and so on. When an analysis is ‘a going concern,’ talking-as-dreaming moves unobtrusively into and out of talking about dreaming. The author provides two detailed clinical examples of analytic work with patients who had very little capacity to dream in the analytic setting. In the first clinical example, talking-as-dreaming served as a form of thinking and relating in which the patient was able for the first time to dream her own (and, in a sense, her father's) formerly unthinkable, undreamable experience. The second clinical example involves the use of talking-as-dreaming as an emotional experience in which the formerly ‘invisible’ patient was able to begin to dream himself into existence. The analyst, while engaging with a patient in talking-as-dreaming, must remain keenly aware that it is critical that the difference in roles of patient and analyst be a continuously felt presence; that the therapeutic goals of analysis be firmly held in mind; and that the patient be given the opportunity to dream himself into existence (as opposed to being dreamt up by the analyst).

responseSet.title: The Coming Changes in Psychoanalytic Education: Part II
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

The Coming Changes in Psychoanalytic Education: Part II

Otto Kernberg

Otto Kernberg

Westchester Division, 21 Bloomingdale Road, White Plains, NY 10605, USA—okernber@med.cornell.edu
(Final version accepted 7 April 2006)

Otto Kernberg  

psychoanalytic education, curriculum, seminars, classroom teaching, institute governance, university relations, admissions criteria, progression, graduation, certification, accreditation, institute-society relations

This paper is the second part of a general analysis of problems in contemporary psychoanalytic education. Having proposed changes in the training analysis and supervisory systems in Part I, here the author focuses on concrete proposals regarding changes in the curriculum, seminars and classroom teaching; the governance of psychoanalytic institutes, relationship of institutes with their respective psychoanalytic society and the role of the university in the development of science and research; the admission, progression, and graduating processes; certification and accreditation.

responseSet.title: ‘Talk to me Baby, Tell me What's the Matter Now’: Semiotic and Developmental Perspectives on Communication in Psychoanalytic Infant Treatment
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

‘Talk to me Baby, Tell me What's the Matter Now’

Semiotic and Developmental Perspectives on Communication in Psychoanalytic Infant Treatment

Björn Salomonsson

Björn Salomonsson

SE-171 76 Stockholm, Sweden—bjorn.salomonsson@ki.se
(Final version accepted 31 March 2006)

Björn Salomonsson  

psychoanalysis of infants and mothers, nonverbal communication, emotional communication, semiotics, symbolization, infant psychotherapy, infant development

Infants suffer to a considerable degree from disturbances in nursing, sleep, mood, and attachment. Psychotherapeutic methods are increasingly used to help them. According to case reports, psychoanalytic work with infants and mothers has shown deep-reaching and often surprisingly rapid results, both in symptom reduction and in improved relations between mother and child. The clinical urgency of the method makes it important to study its results and theoretical underpinnings. Among the theoretical issues often raised in discussions on this modified form of psychoanalysis, those addressing the nature of communication between analyst, baby, and the mother are the most frequent. For example, how and what does an infant understand when the analyst interprets to her? What does the analyst understand of the infant's communication? These issues are addressed by investigating the infant's tools for understanding linguistic and emotional communication, and by providing a semiotic framework for describing the communication between the three participants in the analytic setting. The paper also investigates problems with the traditional ways of using the concept of symbolization within psychoanalytic theory. The theoretical investigation is illustrated by two brief vignettes from psychoanalytic work with an 8 month-old girl and her mother.

responseSet.title: The Predicting Brain: Unconscious Repetition, Conscious Reflection and Therapeutic Change
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

The Predicting Brain: Unconscious Repetition, Conscious Reflection and Therapeutic Change

Regina Pally

Regina Pally

11980 San Vicente Blvd, Suite 810, Los Angeles, CA 90049, USA — rpally@ucla.edu (Final version accepted 12 September 2006)

Regina Pally  

prediction, repetition, subjective reality, transference, consciousness, intentions, empathy, theory of mind

Neuroscience indicates that ‘repetition’ is fundamental to brain function. The brain non-consciously predicts what is most likely to happen and sets in motion perceptions, emotions, behaviors and interpersonal responses best adapted to what is expected— before events occur. Predictions enable individuals to be ready ‘ahead of time’ so reactions occur rapidly and smoothly when events occur. The brain uses past learning as the guide for what to expect in the future. Because of prediction, present experience and responses are shaped by the past. Predictions from early life can be deeply encoded and enduring. Predictions based on the past allow for more efficient brain function in the present, but can lead to mistakes. When what is predicted does not occur, consciousness can be engaged to monitor and correct the situation. But if a perception or emotion seems reasonable for the situation, a person might not notice an error, and a maladaptive ‘repetition’ may remain unchanged. The author discusses how predictions contribute to psychological defenses and transference repetition, and how conscious self-reflection facilitates therapeutic change. The neuroscience of prediction indicates why, in certain cases, active engagement by the analyst may be necessary. The author makes the argument for use of a ‘neuroscience interpretation’.

responseSet.title: Thinking with, and About, Patients Too Scared to Think: Can Non-Interpretive Maneuvers Stimulate Reflective Thought?
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Thinking with, and About, Patients Too Scared to Think

Can Non-Interpretive Maneuvers Stimulate Reflective Thought?

Richard Tuch

Richard Tuch

1800 Fairburn Avenue, Suite 206, Los Angeles, CA 90025, USA—RTuch@aol.com
(Final version accepted 8 March 2006)

Richard Tuch  

reflective thought, concrete thinking, theory of mind, separation anxiety, symbolic thought, insightfulness, multiple perspectives, otherness

Patients incapable of higher-order (symbolic) thinking can often not tolerate evidence of the analyst's separate existence, particularly when that ‘otherness’ becomes evident in the process of the analyst's reflecting upon and interpreting how the patient experiences or represents the analyst. The patient's intolerance of the analyst's efforts to think (reflect upon and interpret) renders the usual psychoanalytic maneuvers employed to stimulate reflective thought ineffective with such patients. Such patients have to learn to tolerate multiple perspectives before they can allow the analyst, or themselves, to think in the other's presence. The author presents two clinical vignettes that illustrate how the analyst's efforts to think about the patient were experienced by the patient as both intolerably distancing and as rejecting of an aspect of the patient's subjective reality. Working psychoanalytically with such patients requires the analyst to forgo the use of narrow interpretations that elucidate unconscious meanings and motives in favor of alternate technical maneuvers capable of facilitating the development of symbolic thinking and reflective thought (insightfulness). These maneuvers include a demonstration of the analyst's willingness and ability to withstand (rather than ‘interpret away’) how he is being psychically represented by the patient, without becoming destroyed by, or lost within, the patient's characterization of him. Beside modeling a tolerance of alternate perspectives of one's self, other non-interpretive maneuvers that help facilitate the development of self-reflective thought include: stimulating the patient's curiosity about the workings of his own mind by identifying incompletely understood behaviors or reactions worthy of greater psychological understanding, and insinuating doubt about the adequacy of the patient's explanations of such phenomena.

responseSet.title: Lacan
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Lacan11

Gilbert Diatkine

Gilbert Diatkine

48 bd Beaumarchais, F-75011 Paris, France — gilbert.diatkine@wanadoo.fr
(Final version accepted 26 June 2005)

Gilbert Diatkine  

short sessions, rapprochement anxiety, jouissance

Jacques Lacan belonged to the second generation of French psychoanalysts which, thanks to the arrival in France of Rudolf Loewenstein, was the first to benefit from a training analysis of sufficient quality and duration. Lacan left the Société Psychanalytique de Paris and the International Psychoanalytic Association in 1953, following a controversy over the short sessions he gave his patients. For Lacan, the anxiety of being absorbed by the object is the principal anxiety from which the anxieties of separation, castration or fragmentation are derived, which may explain why he did not keep his patients for a sufficient length of time. Lacan transformed his difficulty into an advocated technique, which he justified by making a critique of the classical technique. He founded his own international psychoanalytic association, in which selection only occurs when the analyst is already at a very advanced stage in his career (‘the authorization of an analyst can only come from himself’). We are indebted to Lacan for having drawn the attention of analysts to the role of language, and especially of words with a double meaning, in the genesis of interpretation, but his theory of language, founded on the assimilation of psychoanalysis to structural linguistics and anthropology, has collapsed. Many of Lacan's other theoretical contributions, such as the renewed interest in the après-coup, the place of mirror relations in narcissism, the distinction between what he calls jouissance and the orgasm, or between the ‘real’ and reality, have been gradually integrated by analysts who accept neither his technique nor his laxity in training.

responseSet.title: Losing a Training Analyst for Ethical Violations: A Candidate's Perspective
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Losing a Training Analyst for Ethical Violations: A Candidate's Perspective

Elizabeth Wallace

Elizabeth Wallace

Floor, 520 — 13 Ave. SW, Calgary, AB — T2R 0X7, Canada — emw.md@telus.net (Final version accepted 10 January 2007)

Elizabeth Wallace  

ethical violation, candidate—training analyst relationship, boundary violation, ethics investigation, forced termination, institutional reactions

The author presents her experience as the analysand of a training analyst who was investigated and expelled for ethical violations with another patient, including sexual-boundary violations, during her analytic training. While boundary violations by training analysts are not uncommon, the particular trauma experienced by ‘bystanders’ such as candidates and supervisees is not discussed in the literature, nor the response of institutes to the educational problems that are generated. The author illustrates the complications for candidates that arise from the dual roles of training analyst as educator and analyst when he or she faces investigation or censure, including isolation and secrecy, which promote various splits in the candidate, analytic dyad and group, as well as loyalty conflicts. The discussion covers three phases of the author's experience as a candidate—analysand, namely the period encompassing the institute's ethics investigation, the announcement of findings to her and to the institute as a group, and the ensuing individual and group dynamics generated by her analyst's expulsion from the institute and revocation of his medical license. Theoretical perspectives are utilized to understand the group regression, including contamination and contagion fears, which occurred in the wake of the training analyst's expulsion, and the impact of these processes on the candidate, including the pressure to function as a ‘container’ for projections of the group. Implications and recommendations for candidates and institutes are made for dealing helpfully with trainees who are affected by the process of dealing with a training analyst's ethical violations. Short-term and longer-term outcomes of the experience are considered.

responseSet.title: The Case of Stevie
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

The Case of Stevie

Alice Jones

Alice Jones

2308 Sixth St, Berkeley, CA 94710, USA—ajones@idiom.com

Alice Jones  

I was much too far out all my life

And not waving but drowning.

Stevie Smith

I've been seeing Stevie for all of my analytic life. We began when she was 35, days after her diagnosis of leukemia. Soon after we began at once a week, she stopped eating and we increased frequency. She wanted to stay untouched: ‘This should be like taking my car to the mechanic; I drop it off and come back when the work's done.’ Pale and ethereal, she lived by numbers: her weight, calories, miles run, dollars. We proceeded by ultimatum: if you don't eat, I'll put you in the hospital; if you don't come more often, we've gone as far as we can and need to stop. Although her initial prognosis was not good, Stevie's leukemia entered into and stayed in remission. After 9 years of therapy, we began working four times a week and using the couch. After 6 years of analysis, we began working five times a week (3 years ago) and something shifted. This change occurred after the death of Stevie's mother. It also coincided with Stevie's retirement from work in a medical-equipment business, where she appeared to be a capable manager and made a good deal of money.

Stevie is the older of two sisters, 4 years apart. The only story she has of her infancy is that she would cry inconsolably for an hour every afternoon and that her mother, on the advice of the pediatrician, let her cry. With a name chosen before she was born, Stevie felt she was supposed to have been a boy. Her younger sister developed severe asthma, was hospitalized at 2 and ‘nearly died’ several times. Her mother was depressed and remote, telling Stevie she had to be big and need nothing. Stevie became the champion of wanting nothing. When Stevie was 13, her alcoholic father had a cerebral hemorrhage and became disabled. He was never able to work again. Her mother retreated further, lying in her room with the shades drawn.

Beneath a superficial compliance, the early years of therapy were full of sadomasochistic struggles revolving around what Stevie could or couldn't take in from me. The anorexia resolved, but her inability to hear did not. While I knew she was fiercely attached, she could hold little of what I said. Her world was one of emotional lock-down. Any feeling knocked her wildly off balance. She lived like a zombie, frozen in stone, thawing gradually, but retaining her fears. With any easing, I would think, ‘Good, we're moving.’ Stevie would come to the next hour saying that, if anything changed, then someday we would end. Further shutdown would ensue. Many of her dreams contained visual images: eyes, glasses, windows, lenses. She was always the one looking on, never the one seen. Three years ago she dreamed of being inside a house like an igloo. The windows were made of ice. She placed her palm on a pane, held it there, and the ice slowly melted through, allowing light to enter.

[[RunningHead]]

The supervisory field and projective identification11

Germano Filho , Antonio Pires , Gerson Berlim , Raul Hartke and Sérgio Lewkowicz

Sérgio Lewkowicz

Rua Itaqui, 89/202, 90460-140 Porto Alegre—RS, Brazil — vollmer@terra.com.br

Av. Taquara, 110/404, 90460-210 Porto Alegre—RS, Brazil — acjpires@via-rs.net

Rua Tobias da Silva, 149/304, 90570-020 Porto Alegre—RS, Brazil — giberlim@terra.com.br

Rua Itaqui, 98/305, 90460-140 Porto Alegre—RS, Brazil — rahartke@brturbo.com.br

Rua Luciana de Abreu, 267/405, 90570-060 Porto Alegre—RS, Brazil — serlew@terra.com.br
(Final version accepted 23 June 2006)

Sérgio Lewkowicz  

supervision, projective identification, supervisory field

Taking as their starting point the Baranger and Baranger model of the ‘psychoanalytic field’, the authors extend the notion of intersubjectivity in the analytic relationship to the supervision process. They use a practical example of a supervision to show the development of what they term the'supervisory field’, formed from the superimposition of the two fields of analyst-patient and supervisor-supervisee. They emphasize the interplay of projective identifications with objects emanating from the inner world of the patient that are relived in the analytic relationship and transposed to the supervisory field. They believe that the concept of the'supervisory field’ contributes to a deeper understanding of the unconscious processes occurring in the mind of analysand, supervisee and supervisor during supervision, particularly regarding the identification, comprehension and resolution of persistent disturbances in the supervisory process.

responseSet.title: Playing with Reality: IV. A Theory of External Reality Rooted in Intersubjectivity
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Playing with Reality: IV. A Theory of External Reality Rooted in Intersubjectivity

Peter Fonagy and Mary Target

Mary Target

Gower Street, London, WC1E 6BT, UK — p.fonagy@ucl.ac.uk
(Final version accepted 25 September 2006)

Mary Target  

external reality, intersubjectivity, infant development

The authors explore the interpersonal aspects of the early development of an experience of external reality and the roots of this experience in primary intersubjectivity. They suggest some implications that this has for psychoanalytic work with the patient's experience of external reality. They argue that the external world is not an independently existing ‘given’, for the infant to discover, as is sometimes implicitly assumed. Infants acquire knowledge about the world not just through their own explorations of it but by using other minds as teachers. The experience of external reality is invariably shaped through subjectivities. The authors argue that at first the infant assumes that his knowledge is knowledge held by all, that what he knows is known by others and that what is known by others is accessible to him. Only slowly does the uniqueness of his own perspective differentiate so that a sense of mental self can develop. In clinical work we frequently observe the undoing of this process of differentiation, and understanding the underlying mechanisms can be helpful in managing the transference and countertransference consequences when the process has been derailed.

responseSet.title: Object Loss, Renewed Mourning, and Psychic Change in Jane Austen's Persuasion
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Object Loss, Renewed Mourning, and Psychic Change in Jane Austen's Persuasion

Margaret Hanly

Margaret Hanly

106 Elm Av, Toronto, ON — M4W 1P2, Canada — cema.hanly@utoronto.ca
(Final version accepted 25 August 2006)

Margaret Hanly  

Jane Austen, mourning, object loss, psychic change, projection, Persuasion

Austen's extraordinary realism in depicting the dynamic internal processes which follow on the heroine's loss in Persuasion becomes clear in the light of a psycho-analytic understanding of mourning. Persuasion dramatizes the effects of a mother's death in adolescence as these come into play at the time of the heroine's separation from her fiancée and her later mourning. The thesis of this paper is that, despite falling in love with the brilliant hero, an unfinished mourning and an unconscious identification with her dead mother helped to persuade the heroine Anne Elliot to break her engagement, to create a “final parting' as her mother had done to her in dying. The heroine's internal monologues show that she has projected some of the darker feelings of mourning, her anger and resentment, on to the hero and that she reopens a complex mourning process, partly through the displacement of affect, showing how traumatic effects of loss can be worked through in deferred action, effecting positive psychic change.

Reply to Drs Mancia and Pugh

Rachel Blass and Zvi Carmeli

Zvi Carmeli

Mt Scopus, Jerusalem 91905, Israel —
rblass@mscc.huji.ac.il;

Mt Scopus, Jerusalem 91905, Israel
zcarmeli@mscc.huji.ac.il

Zvi Carmeli  

Dear Editors,

In Blass and Carmeli (2007), we argued that the nature of the relationship between biology and psychoanalysis is such that neuroscientific findings (however valid or true they may be) are irrelevant to psychoanalysis and that claims that neuroscience is relevant to psychoanalysis rest on fallacies. We illustrated this by

1068

studying a representative selection of writings, all recently appearing in official psychoanalytic contexts, and written by well-known psychoanalysts or experts in neuropsychoanalysis.

The letters of Mancia and Pugh offer assertions of their authors' deep conviction in what they consider the obvious value of neuropsychoanalysis. They suggest that to reject the relevance of neuroscience to psychoanalysis is to reject interdisciplinary dialogue, progress and knowledge itself. However, they do not justify these assertions and assumptions and fail to present arguments or evidence that would demonstrate that our position is mistaken.

More specifically, the letters are primarily comprised of three kinds of statements: first, there are numerous simple positive assertions, presented without any supporting arguments, that the integration of neuroscientific observations if properly applied does not ‘pose any threat to psychoanalysis’ and that it has already produced ‘highly significant and far-reaching’ results. In our paper, we pointed to a misguided intuition that underlies the idea that neuroscientific findings regarding memory can contribute to psychoanalysis. Mancia cites this intuition, ignores our detailed arguments regarding why it cannot be relied on and simply asserts that he ‘find[s] it obvious’ and is disturbed that ‘this does not seem so clear to the authors'. Similarly, Pugh states that it would be ‘absurd’ for psychoanalysis not to take note of the findings of neuroscience but he does not really explain why. Indeed he mentions what in his estimation are important discoveries made possible by neuroscience (e.g. of the unrepressed unconscious). But to simply mention such contributions without countering our arguments that show that the kind of thinking that supports them is misguided is to beg the question.

responseSet.title: Supportive Interventions and Nonsymbolic Mental Functioning
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Supportive Interventions and Nonsymbolic Mental Functioning

Serge Lecours

Serge Lecours

Département de Psychologie, Université de Montréal, CP 6128, succ. Centre-ville, Montréal, QC H3C 3J7, Canada — serge.lecours@umontreal.ca (Final version accepted 18 September 2006)

Serge Lecours  

supportive interventions, symbolization, symbolic and nonsymbolic mental functioning, therapeutic action, mental procedures, interpretation, psychoanalytic psychotherapy, Bion

The author aims to show how supportive interventions are the analyst's most relevant therapeutic means to helping patients with a feeble symbolic system transform nonsymbolic episodes and reestablish symbolic mental functioning. Symbolic and nonsymbolic modes of mental functioning are first outlined. Supportive interventions are redefined as an analyst's effort at improving a patient's nonsymbolic mental functioning, by using principally pragmatic or interactive aspects of communication to deal with her or his patient's nonsymbolic in-session experiences. These interventions are psychoanalytic when transference focused, in so far as they foster the symbolization and transformation of more primitive (nonsymbolic) layers of the transference. Some probable mechanisms underlying the effect of supportive interventions on nonsymbolic functioning include the modification of mental procedures. Supportive interventions also help restore symbolic mental elaboration through the gratification of a basic ego or self-need, bringing about a temporary relief from psychic pain, with increased affect tolerance and a renewed capacity to use symbols. This soothing effect accounts for a missing link in Bion's model of the elaborative effect of the analyst's reverie.

responseSet.title: Marcel Duchamp: On the Fruitful use of Narcissism and Destructiveness in Contemporary Art
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Marcel Duchamp: On the Fruitful use of Narcissism and Destructiveness in Contemporary Art

Adela Abella

Adela Abella

217 rte d'Annecy, CH-1257 La Croix-de-Rozon, Switzerland — adela.abella@sezenove.ch
(Final version accepted 21 September 2006)

Adela Abella  

narcissism, destructiveness, art

Considering Marcel Duchamp's work, this paper raises the question as to the nature of the subjective experience proposed by contemporary art to today's audience. Approaching art through the concept of sublimation, Freud maintains a fundamentally optimistic and positive view, putting forward its libidinal and sexual aspects, the pursuit of pleasure, beauty, and omnipotence. Following the path opened by Freud through the concept of the “uncanny', most post-Freudian authors have proposed a “blacker' image of artistic endeavour, allowing the expression of aggression. From a perspective which is neither that of an art historian nor a moralist, the author proposes the idea that certain propositions of contemporary art may allow the viewer to live narcissistic and destructive fantasies, via culturally sanctioned and socially acceptable means. The recognition of the fertile use of destruction as a condition of the emergence of the new, on the one hand, as well as the legitimacy of the expression through art of the most primitive fantasies and the right to non-communication, on the other, are postulated as constructs for a non-normative, non-judgemental psychoanalytic approach to the cultural world.
